sourceDetails.organization: Leon Festinger and James M. Carlsmith, Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology
sourceDetails.whyReputable: The landmark peer-reviewed primary experiment behind the insufficient-justification example.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Preserved the primary study while removing inevitability.

memoryAids.title: Prejudice & Discrimination Interventions
memoryAids.content: **"CSER"** — Four approaches that reduce prejudice: 🤝 **C**ontact hypothesis (Allport) — Groups must interact with: → Equal status, common goals, cooperation, institutional support 📖 **S**uperordinate goals (Sherif) — Robbers Cave study → Shared goals that REQUIRE cooperation reduce intergroup conflict 🧠 **E**mpathy training — Perspective-taking exercises → Jane Elliott's blue eyes/brown eyes experiment 🏫 **R**ecategorization — Expand "us" to include "them" → Common in-group identity model

memoryAids.title: Ainsworth Strange Situation
memoryAids.content: **Strange Situation reunion patterns — describe observed relationship behavior, not fixed child diagnoses:** 🟢 **Secure:** seeks or accepts comfort and returns to exploration 🔵 **Avoidant:** minimizes overt proximity-seeking or avoids reunion contact 🟡 **Resistant/ambivalent:** seeks contact while resisting or remaining difficult to soothe 🔴 **Disorganized:** contradictory, apprehensive, freezing, or disoriented behavior without a coherent strategy Patterns are interpreted from the full standardized procedure. They do not prove a caregiver trait, maltreatment, or the child's behavior in every relationship. Culture, context, stress, and measurement conditions matter.

sourceDetails.title: Psychology 2e, Section 9.3: Stages of Development
sourceDetails.whyReputable: An expert-authored, peer-reviewed textbook from nonprofit Rice University.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed deterministic caregiver labels and prevalence claims; clarified relationship-specific observational classification.

memoryAids.title: Freud's Psychosexual Stages
memoryAids.content: **"Old Age Produces Lots of Gas"** (with key fixation outcomes): 👶 **O**ral (0-1): Mouth focus → Fixation: smoking, overeating, dependency 🚽 **A**nal (1-3): Toilet training → Fixation: anal-retentive (orderly) or anal-expulsive (messy) 👀 **P**hallic (3-6): Oedipus/Electra complex → Fixation: identity/sexuality issues 📚 **L**atency (6-puberty): Sexual urges dormant → Focus on social/academic skills 💑 **G**enital (puberty+): Mature sexuality → Healthy = balanced adult relationships **Key:** Oedipus complex (boys) vs. Electra complex (girls) — phallic stage → Resolution through identification with same-sex parent

memoryAids.title: Kubler-Ross Grief Stages
memoryAids.content: **DABDA is a historical vocabulary, not a required sequence or timetable for bereavement.** Denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance described responses associated with confronting death and were later widely applied to grief. A person may experience some, none, several at once, or revisit feelings without progressing through stages. Culture, relationship, circumstances of the death, development, health, support, and prior loss shape grieving. Do not tell a bereaved person which stage they are in or treat continuing sadness as failure to reach acceptance. Distinguish varied, often oscillating grief from DSM-5-TR prolonged grief disorder, which requires its own duration, symptoms, impairment, developmental, and cultural-context criteria.

sourceDetails.title: Stages of Grief Portrayed on the Internet: A Systematic Analysis and Critical Appraisal
sourceDetails.organization: Frontiers in Psychology, indexed in PubMed Central
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A peer-reviewed critical analysis documenting how prescriptive stage presentations can misrepresent and harmfully constrain grief.

sourceDetails.title: DSM-5-TR: Prolonged Grief Disorder
sourceDetails.whyReputable: The official psychiatric association fact sheet explaining the evidence-reviewed DSM-5-TR diagnostic addition and its core features.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Recast DABDA as nonprescriptive history and separated normal variability in grief from a formal diagnosis.

memoryAids.title: Temperament (Thomas & Chess)
memoryAids.content: **3 temperament types — "ESD":** 😊 **E**asy (40%): Regular rhythms, positive mood, adaptable → Most common and easiest to parent 😤 **D**ifficult (10%): Irregular, intense negative reactions, slow to adapt → Higher risk for behavior problems IF poor fit 😐 **S**low-to-Warm-Up (15%): Mildly negative to new, gradually adapts → Needs patient, low-pressure approach 35% don't fit neatly into categories **Goodness of fit** = Match between child's temperament & caregiver's style → MOST important factor for outcomes

memoryAids.title: DSM-5 Schizophrenia Criteria
memoryAids.content: **DSM-5-TR retrieval cue: symptoms + function + course + exclusions, never one symptom or a dopamine story.** The active-phase cluster requires at least two of delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, grossly disorganized or catatonic behavior, and negative symptoms for a significant portion of one month, with at least one of the first three. Continuous disturbance lasts at least six months and includes the active phase; functioning is markedly below the prior level in major areas. Evaluate mood episodes, substances, medications, neurological or medical causes, developmental history, and cultural context. Hallucinations and unusual beliefs occur in multiple conditions, and schizophrenia is not dissociative identity disorder. Use the current DSM-5-TR for exact wording, coding, and exceptions.

sourceDetails.title: Treatments for Schizophrenia in Adults: A Systematic Review, Evidence Summary
sourceDetails.organization: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality, hosted by NCBI Bookshelf
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A U.S. federal comparative-effectiveness review that accurately summarizes the DSM symptom and duration structure.

sourceDetails.title: Schizophrenia
sourceDetails.organization: National Institute of Mental Health, National Institutes of Health
sourceDetails.whyReputable: An official federal resource describing psychotic, negative, and cognitive symptoms, differential context, and recovery-oriented treatment.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Added functional, course, exclusion, developmental, and cultural gates and rejected single-symptom diagnosis.

memoryAids.title: DSM-5 Major Depression
memoryAids.content: **SIGECAPS is only a symptom-retrieval cue; diagnosis requires threshold, change, impairment, exclusions, and context.** A major depressive episode involves at least five symptoms during the same two-week period, representing a change from prior functioning, with depressed mood or diminished interest or pleasure among them. Symptoms include sleep, appetite or weight, psychomotor, energy, guilt or worthlessness, concentration or decision-making, and death or suicidal-thinking changes, but exact frequency and developmental wording matter. Establish clinically significant distress or impairment and assess bipolar history, substances, medications, medical conditions, psychosis, grief, culture, and safety. Bereavement does not automatically exclude an episode, and sadness alone does not establish one. Use current DSM-5-TR criteria rather than the mnemonic for diagnosis.

sourceDetails.title: DSM-5 Major Depressive Episode
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A medically reviewed NCBI-hosted criteria table specifying the symptom threshold, two-week period, change, and impairment requirements.

sourceDetails.title: Depression
sourceDetails.organization: National Institute of Mental Health, National Institutes of Health
sourceDetails.whyReputable: An official federal resource addressing symptom variation, medical mimics, treatment, functional impact, and help-seeking.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Kept the symptom cue but added change, impairment, differential diagnosis, bipolar, grief, culture, and safety requirements.

memoryAids.title: DSM-5 Personality Clusters
memoryAids.content: **"Weird, Wild, Worried"** — The 3 personality disorder clusters: 🌀 **Cluster A** = **Weird** (odd/eccentric): → **P**aranoid, **S**chizoid, **S**chizotypal → "PSS" — they're suspicious/withdrawn 🔥 **Cluster B** = **Wild** (dramatic/emotional): → **A**ntisocial, **B**orderline, **H**istrionic, **N**arcissistic → "ABHN" — they're all about attention 😰 **Cluster C** = **Worried** (anxious/fearful): → **A**voidant, **D**ependent, **O**bsessive-Compulsive → "ADO" — they're afraid of things **Most tested:** Borderline (unstable relationships, splitting, self-harm) & Antisocial (conduct disorder before 18, lack of remorse)

memoryAids.title: Wechsler vs Stanford-Binet
memoryAids.content: **"Wechsler = Various versions, Stanford = Single tradition"** 📐 **Wechsler scales** (most widely used IQ tests): → WAIS (adults), WISC (children 6-16), WPPSI (preschool 2.5-7) → Mean = 100, SD = **15** → Scores: FSIQ + Index scores (Verbal Comp, Perceptual, Working Memory, Processing Speed) → **Deviation IQ** (not ratio IQ) 📊 **Stanford-Binet 5:** → Ages 2-85+, one test for all ages → Mean = 100, SD = **15** (same as Wechsler since SB5) → 5 factors: Fluid, Knowledge, Quantitative, Visual-Spatial, Working Memory **Key distinction:** Wechsler has index scores and is most used clinically. Stanford-Binet better for very high/low IQ.

memoryAids.title: Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV
memoryAids.content: **Read the 2?2 table:** Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN), among people with the condition who tests positive. Specificity = TN/(TN+FP), among people without it who tests negative. PPV = TP/(TP+FP), among positive results who has the condition. NPV = TN/(TN+FN), among negative results who does not. Sensitivity and specificity can vary with threshold, spectrum, setting, and verification. Predictive values depend strongly on prevalence: the same test can have much lower PPV in a low-prevalence population.

sourceDetails.title: Principles of Epidemiology in Public Health Practice, Third Edition: Lesson 3
sourceDetails.organization: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
sourceDetails.whyReputable: The U.S. federal public-health authority?s training text for diagnostic-test measures.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Added exact denominators and context/base-rate boundaries.

memoryAids.title: PTSD Diagnostic Criteria
memoryAids.content: **"TRAID"** — PTSD requires all of these: 🔴 **T**raumatic event — Exposure to death, serious injury, or sexual violence (experienced, witnessed, learned about, or repeated exposure) 🔁 **R**e-experiencing — Intrusions, nightmares, flashbacks 🚫 **A**voidance — Avoiding trauma-related thoughts, feelings, reminders 😶 **I**mpaired cognition/mood — Negative beliefs, detachment, blame 💥 **D**ysregulated arousal — Hypervigilance, startle, sleep problems, irritability **Duration:** 1+ month (if < 1 month = Acute Stress Disorder) **Onset specifier:** Delayed if onset 6+ months after trauma

memoryAids.title: Systematic Desensitization
memoryAids.content: **Wolpe's 3-step recipe** — Like slowly warming up a cold pool: 1. 🧘 **Relaxation training** — Learn deep muscle relaxation (can't be anxious AND relaxed) 2. 📝 **Anxiety hierarchy** — Rank fears from least to most (SUDs 0-100) 3. 🔄 **Gradual pairing** — Relax + imagine lowest fear → work up the ladder Based on **reciprocal inhibition**: You can't feel relaxed and anxious simultaneously. **Key distinction:** → Systematic desensitization = GRADUAL + IMAGINAL (usually) → Flooding = ALL AT ONCE + IN VIVO (usually) → In vivo desensitization = GRADUAL + REAL exposure

memoryAids.title: Ellis REBT — ABCDE Model
memoryAids.content: **"ABCDE"** — The core of Rational Emotive Behavior Therapy: 🅰️ **A**ctivating event — What happened ("I failed the test") 🅱️ **B**elief — Your interpretation ("I MUST succeed or I'm worthless") 🅰️ **C**onsequence — Emotional result (depression, anxiety) ✋ **D**ispute — Challenge the irrational belief ("Says who? Where's the evidence?") ✅ **E**ffective new philosophy — "I prefer to succeed, but failure doesn't define me" **Ellis's 3 musts:** 1. "I MUST do well" → anxiety 2. "Others MUST treat me well" → anger 3. "Life MUST be easy" → low frustration tolerance **REBT vs. Beck's CT:** REBT is more directive, confrontational, philosophical

memoryAids.title: DBT Skills — Linehan
memoryAids.content: **4 DBT modules** = **"MIDER"** (Mi-Der): 🧘 **M**indfulness — Present-moment awareness (core skill) 👥 **I**nterpersonal Effectiveness — DEAR MAN (Describe, Express, Assert, Reinforce, Mindful, Appear confident, Negotiate) ⚖️ **D**istress Tolerance — TIPP (Temperature, Intense exercise, Paced breathing, Paired muscle relaxation) 🎭 **E**motion **R**egulation — Opposite action, checking the facts **Originally developed for:** Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD) **Key concept:** Dialectical = balancing ACCEPTANCE and CHANGE → "I accept myself as I am AND I want to change"

memoryAids.title: Gestalt Therapy Techniques
memoryAids.content: **"EHE" — Perls' Gestalt techniques:** 🪑 **E**mpty Chair — Talk to an imagined person in an empty chair → Brings unfinished business to the present moment 🤚 **E**xaggeration — Amplify a gesture, phrase, or feeling → "Say that louder!" "Do that movement bigger!" 🔄 **H**ere-and-Now focus — "What are you experiencing RIGHT NOW?" → Change "I can't" to "I won't" (taking responsibility) → Change "Why?" questions to "What/How?" **Core principle:** Awareness IS curative → "Lose your mind and come to your senses" — Fritz Perls **Unfinished business** = lingering feelings from past that block present functioning

memoryAids.title: Motivational Interviewing
memoryAids.content: **MI spirit: partnership, acceptance, compassion, and evocation. Processes: engaging ? focusing ? evoking ? planning.** OARS?open questions, affirmations, reflections, summaries?supports conversation, but MI is not a bag of techniques. The practitioner listens for and strengthens the person?s own language for change while respecting autonomy; sustain talk is explored rather than argued away. Planning follows sufficient readiness and shared focus. MI is collaborative and directional, not persuasion by stealth or a guarantee of change.

sourceDetails.title: Glossary of Motivational Interviewing Terms
sourceDetails.whyReputable: Terminology from the international professional network devoted to fidelity-consistent MI.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Aligned the cue with current MI spirit and processes.

memoryAids.title: Solution-Focused Brief Therapy
memoryAids.content: **3 Key Questions — The SFBT Toolkit:** 🪄 **Miracle Question** — "If a miracle happened tonight and your problem was solved, what would be different tomorrow?" → Helps visualize goals concretely 📐 **Scaling Question** — "On a scale of 1-10, where are you now?" → Measures progress, identifies small wins 💡 **Exception Question** — "When was a time the problem DIDN'T happen?" → Identifies existing strengths and solutions **Key principle:** Clients already have solutions — help them find them! **Focus on WHAT WORKS, not what's broken** **De Shazer & Berg** — developed in the 1980s

memoryAids.title: Family Therapy Models
memoryAids.content: **"MSBNE"** — Five major family therapy approaches: 🗺️ **M**inuchin = **S**tructural — Boundaries, subsystems, hierarchy → Join, restructure, boundary-making. **Enmeshed** vs **Disengaged** 🔄 **S**alvador Minuchin (same) = Focus on STRUCTURE of the family 📐 **B**owen = **M**ultigenerational — Differentiation of self, triangulation → Genogram, emotional cutoff, family projection process 💬 **S**atir = Experiential/Communication — 4 communication stances: → Blamer, Placater, Super-reasonable, Irrelevant 🎯 **Haley** = **S**trategic — Directives, paradoxical intention → Focus on presenting problem, power dynamics

memoryAids.title: Group Therapy Stages
memoryAids.content: **Tuckman's stages — "Funny Nuns Storm, Then Perform, Adjourn":** 1. **F**orming — Orientation, dependence on leader, politeness 2. **S**torming — Conflict, resistance, power struggles 3. **N**orming — Cohesion develops, rules established 4. **P**erforming — Productive work, collaboration 5. **A**djourning — Termination, separation issues **Yalom's Therapeutic Factors** (most tested): → **Universality** — "I'm not alone" (huge in groups!) → **Altruism** — Helping others helps yourself → **Catharsis** — Emotional release → **Group cohesiveness** — Bonding, trust → **Interpersonal learning** — The group IS the therapy → **Instillation of hope** — Seeing others improve

memoryAids.title: Threats to Internal Validity
memoryAids.content: **Internal validity asks: could a plausible alternative explain the observed effect?** Common threats include selection/confounding, history, maturation, testing, instrumentation, regression to the mean, differential attrition or missingness, deviations from assignment, contamination, and outcome-assessment bias. Random assignment helps balance confounders in expectation, but does not fix attrition, nonadherence, measurement change, contamination, selective reporting, or chance imbalance. Use design-matched protections: comparison groups, allocation concealment, blinding where feasible, standardized measurement, fidelity tracking, prespecified analyses, and principled missing-data/sensitivity analyses. "Hawthorne effect" is not a universal explanation.

sourceDetails.title: What Works Clearinghouse Procedures and Standards Handbook, Version 5.0
sourceDetails.organization: Institute of Education Sciences, U.S. Department of Education
sourceDetails.whyReputable: Official federal evidence standards detailing design features and risks to causal inference.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Reorganized the mnemonic around causal alternatives and corrected the claim that randomization fixes most threats.

memoryAids.title: Threats to External Validity
memoryAids.content: **"Can I Generalize?"** — External validity = generalizability 🧪 **Reactive effects of testing** — Pretest sensitizes participants 🏫 **Reactive effects of setting** — Lab ≠ real world 👥 **Selection-treatment interaction** — Results only for this sample type 📋 **Multiple treatment interference** — Carryover effects **Key distinction:** → **Internal validity** = Did X cause Y? (causal confidence) → **External validity** = Does it apply elsewhere? (generalizability) Increase one → often decrease the other **Most controlled = most artificial**

memoryAids.title: ANOVA Types
memoryAids.content: **Pick your ANOVA by counting IVs and DVs:** 📊 **One-way ANOVA** — 1 IV (3+ groups), 1 DV → Ex: Comparing 3 therapy types on depression scores 📊 **Factorial ANOVA** — 2+ IVs, 1 DV → Ex: Therapy type × Gender on depression (2×3 factorial) → Tests MAIN effects AND INTERACTION effects 📊 **Repeated-measures ANOVA** — Same subjects measured multiple times → Like within-subjects design 📊 **ANCOVA** — ANOVA + covariate (controls for a confound) → Ex: Control for pre-test score when comparing therapies 📊 **MANOVA** — Multiple DVs → Ex: Therapy effects on depression AND anxiety **Post-hoc tests** (Tukey, Bonferroni) → tell you WHICH groups differ

memoryAids.title: Correlation vs Regression
memoryAids.content: **Correlation** = Do X and Y move together? (strength + direction) → r ranges from −1 to +1 → r² = coefficient of determination (% variance explained) → CANNOT infer causation! **Regression** = Can I PREDICT Y from X? (builds an equation) → Simple: 1 predictor → Y = a + bX → Multiple: 2+ predictors → Y = a + b₁X₁ + b₂X₂... → Uses beta weights to show relative contribution **Memory trick:** → Correlation asks "ARE they related?" → Regression asks "HOW MUCH does X predict Y?" → Multiple regression = the MOST common multivariate technique on the EPPP

memoryAids.title: Parametric Test Assumptions
memoryAids.content: **"NIHR"** — 4 assumptions for parametric tests (t-test, ANOVA): 📊 **N**ormality — Data is normally distributed (bell curve) 🔢 **I**nterval/ratio data — Continuous, meaningful numbers 🏠 **H**omogeneity of variance — Equal spread in each group 🎲 **R**andom/independent sampling — Observations are independent **If violated** → Use non-parametric alternatives: → t-test → Mann-Whitney U (independent) or Wilcoxon (paired) → ANOVA → Kruskal-Wallis → Pearson r → Spearman rho → Chi-square = already non-parametric (nominal data)

memoryAids.title: Standard Error of Measurement
memoryAids.content: **Classical-test-theory SEM** is a score-unit estimate of measurement error, often approximated as SD ? ?(1 ? reliability) for the relevant distribution and reliability estimate. Higher reliability generally yields smaller SEM with other terms fixed. A score interval needs an appropriate multiplier and assumptions; ??1 SEM guarantees a 68% true-score range? is too strong. Precision can vary across scores, forms, groups, and purposes. Comparing scores requires the standard error of the difference and a decision rule?not simply a difference greater than one SEM.

sourceDetails.title: NCME Glossary of Important Assessment and Measurement Terms
sourceDetails.organization: National Council on Measurement in Education
sourceDetails.whyReputable: Field-standard definitions from a leading professional measurement organization.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Corrected confidence-interval and score-comparison shortcuts.

memoryAids.title: Regression to the Mean
memoryAids.content: **It's like flipping coins:** If you flip 10 heads in a row (extreme score), your NEXT set of flips will likely be closer to 5 heads (the mean). **In research:** → If you select people with EXTREME pretest scores... → Their posttest scores will naturally move toward the average → This is NOT because your treatment worked! **Example:** Kids with the lowest reading scores → retested → scores improved. But that's partly regression, not just tutoring! **Fix:** Use a control group! Both groups regress, so the DIFFERENCE between groups shows the real effect.

memoryAids.title: Informed Consent Elements
memoryAids.content: **"PRNDCV"** — 6 elements of informed consent ("Print DC V"): 📋 **P**urpose — Nature and purpose of therapy/assessment ⚠️ **R**isks — Potential risks and discomfort 🎯 **N**o obligation — Right to withdraw at ANY time 📏 **D**uration & procedures — What will happen and how long 💰 **C**osts — Fees and financial arrangements 🔒 **V**oluntary — Free of coercion; capacity to consent **Special populations:** → Minors: Parent/guardian consent + child's ASSENT → Mandated clients: Still get informed consent (limits apply) → Must inform about limits of confidentiality at START

memoryAids.title: Multiple Relationships (Dual)
memoryAids.content: **A multiple relationship is not automatically unethical, but "not forbidden" is not the end of the analysis.** APA Standard 3.05 includes another role with the same person, a relationship with someone closely associated with that person, or a promise of a future relationship. Avoid it when reasonably expected to impair objectivity, competence, or effectiveness, or risk exploitation or harm. If an unforeseen multiple relationship develops, take reasonable steps to resolve it with due regard for the affected person and Code compliance. Context matters in rural, military, disability, and community settings. Sexual relationships with current therapy clients are prohibited; former-client relationships have a two-year absolute minimum plus exceptional-circumstance safeguards, not automatic permission after two years.

sourceDetails.title: Ethical Principles of Psychologists and Code of Conduct
sourceDetails.whyReputable: APA's official primary text for ethical decision context, multiple relationships, avoiding harm, consultation, and sexual-boundary standards.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Used the Code definition and impairment/exploitation test while correcting the after-two-years-is-allowed shortcut.

memoryAids.title: Tarasoff & Duty to Protect
memoryAids.content: **Tarasoff is the historical California anchor; the controlling duty is current jurisdiction-specific law and policy.** California Civil Code section 43.92 addresses a patient-communicated serious threat of physical violence against a reasonably identifiable victim and describes reasonable efforts to communicate with the victim and law enforcement. Other jurisdictions may impose, permit, or not specify warning or protective action, define covered professionals and triggering information differently, and recognize case law as well as statutes. Do not require the words "credible" or "imminent" unless the governing authority does. Clinically assess the threat and context, consult promptly without unnecessary disclosure, follow emergency and organizational procedures, take legally authorized protective steps, and document facts, authority, consultation, reasoning, actions, and follow-up.

sourceDetails.title: California Civil Code section 43.92
sourceDetails.whyReputable: The official current statutory text for California's psychotherapist duty-to-protect liability rule and specified protective efforts.

sourceDetails.title: The Tarasoff Rule: The Implications of Interstate Variation and Gaps in Professional Training
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A peer-reviewed law-and-psychiatry review documenting mandatory, permissive, common-law, and unspecified interstate approaches.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed a universal trigger and checklist, quoted California's actual rule, and centered current jurisdiction-specific authority.

memoryAids.title: Record Keeping & Documentation
memoryAids.content: **"SOAPIER"** — Documentation format: 📋 **S**ubjective — Client's self-report ("I feel hopeless") 🔍 **O**bjective — Clinician's observations (flat affect, tearful) 📊 **A**ssessment — Clinical impression/diagnosis 📝 **P**lan — Treatment plan, next steps 📌 **I**ntervention — What you DID in session 📈 **E**valuation — How is treatment working? 🔄 **R**evision — Changes to the plan **APA guidelines:** → Keep records for **7 years** after last contact (3 years after minor reaches 18) → Records belong to the PROVIDER (not client), but client has right to ACCESS → Write as if records will be read in court

memoryAids.title: Termination Ethics
memoryAids.content: **When to terminate — "PAIN":** 🎯 **P**rogress — Client has met treatment goals (ideal!) ⚠️ **A**bsence of benefit — Therapy is not helping 🚫 **I**nappropriate continuation — Client is being harmed 🚶 **N**eeds not met — Beyond your competence → REFER **Key rules:** → NEVER terminate because client can't pay (arrange alternatives) → NEVER terminate to start a sexual relationship → Pre-termination counseling is REQUIRED → Provide referrals when appropriate → Abandonment = failing to arrange for a client's care **Managed care:** Even if insurer limits sessions, YOU still have ethical obligations

memoryAids.title: GAD vs Panic vs Phobia
memoryAids.content: **Anxiety Disorder Quick Sort:** 😰 **GAD** = Worrying about EVERYTHING for 6+ months → 3+ physical symptoms (restlessness, fatigue, tension, concentration, irritability, sleep) → "What if..." thinking about MULTIPLE areas 💥 **Panic Disorder** = Recurrent unexpected PANIC ATTACKS → 4+ symptoms peaking in MINUTES → Plus fear of HAVING ANOTHER attack (1+ month) 🕷️ **Specific Phobia** = Marked fear of a SPECIFIC object/situation → Immediately provoked, avoided, out of proportion → 6+ months 🏙️ **Agoraphobia** = Fear of 2+ of: public transport, open spaces, enclosed space, crowds, being outside alone → Separate diagnosis from panic in DSM-5!

memoryAids.title: BPD Criteria
memoryAids.content: **Borderline personality disorder is a pervasive pattern involving instability in relationships, self-image, affect, and impulsivity.** Mnemonics can cue criteria but do not replace a longitudinal, culturally informed assessment or differential diagnosis. Common criterion areas include abandonment sensitivity, unstable intense relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, suicidal or self-injurious behavior, affective instability, chronic emptiness, intense anger, and transient stress-related paranoia or dissociation. Dialectical behavior therapy has strong evidence for recurrent self-harm and emotion dysregulation and was originally developed for chronically suicidal people with BPD. Other structured treatments also have evidence; “gold standard” should not imply one universally superior treatment.

sourceDetails.title: Borderline Personality Disorder
sourceDetails.organization: National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH), National Institutes of Health
sourceDetails.whyReputable: The U.S. federal agency leading mental-health research provides this evidence-grounded overview.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed a universal gold-standard claim and clarified that multiple structured psychotherapies have evidence.

memoryAids.title: Token Economy & Contingency Management
memoryAids.content: **Token Economy = Points for prizes — operant conditioning in action!** Based on: **Secondary reinforcement** (tokens become reinforcing through pairing with primary reinforcers) **3 key components:** 1. Target behaviors clearly DEFINED 2. Tokens earned for target behaviors 3. Tokens exchanged for backup reinforcers (privileges, items) **Most used with:** → Psychiatric inpatients → Classrooms → Group homes / residential treatment **Contingency contract** = Written agreement specifying behaviors, reinforcers, and consequences → Used in family therapy, couples therapy, schools → Both parties agree and sign

memoryAids.title: Single-Subject Research Designs
memoryAids.content: **ABA Designs — The Behavioral Gold Standard:** 📊 **A-B** = Baseline → Treatment (weakest — no reversal) 📊 **A-B-A** = Baseline → Treatment → Withdrawal → If behavior returns to baseline = treatment caused change → Ethical concern: ending on no-treatment phase! 📊 **A-B-A-B** = Best! Replicates the effect → Baseline → Treatment → Withdrawal → Treatment → "Two demonstrations of effect" 📊 **Multiple Baseline** = Across behaviors, settings, or subjects → Stagger treatment introduction (no need for withdrawal!) → Used when reversal is unethical/impossible **Key:** Visual analysis (not inferential stats) is primary for single-subject designs

memoryAids.title: Bystander Effect & Diffusion of Responsibility
memoryAids.content: **Bystander effect = a probabilistic group-size effect, not "more people always means less help."** Possible decision points include noticing, interpreting need, accepting responsibility, knowing an action, and acting. **Diffusion of responsibility, pluralistic ignorance, and evaluation concerns** can inhibit intervention. Meta-analysis found an overall bystander effect, but perceived danger, perpetrator presence, costs, competence, and relationships moderate it; dangerous emergencies can attenuate or reverse the pattern. In real safety situations, contact emergency services and give a specific bystander a concrete task rather than relying on a classroom slogan. The popular Kitty Genovese narrative omitted or distorted important facts.

sourceDetails.title: The bystander-effect: a meta-analytic review on bystander intervention in dangerous and non-dangerous emergencies
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A peer-reviewed meta-analysis that quantifies the overall effect and identifies important moderators, including perceived danger.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Replaced an absolute rule with the meta-analytic effect, moderators, and a safe action cue.

memoryAids.title: Psychoanalytic Key Concepts
memoryAids.content: **Freud's Structural Model — "Super-ego, Ego, Id"** 👹 **Id** = Pleasure principle — "I WANT it NOW!" → Unconscious, instincts, libido, thanatos 🧠 **Ego** = Reality principle — "Let me figure out a realistic way" → Mostly conscious, mediates between id & superego 👼 **Superego** = Morality principle — "But is it RIGHT?" → Internalized parental/societal values, conscience **Psychoanalytic techniques:** → **Free association** — Say whatever comes to mind → **Dream analysis** — Manifest (surface) vs latent (hidden) content → **Transference** — Client projects feelings onto therapist → **Interpretation** — Therapist reveals unconscious meaning → **Resistance** — Blocking of unconscious material

memoryAids.title: Supervision Models
memoryAids.content: **3 main supervision models:** 📚 **Developmental** (Stoltenberg & Delworth): → Supervisee grows through stages (Level 1→2→3→3i) → L1: Anxious, dependent, rigid → needs structure → L2: Confusion, fluctuation → growing autonomy → L3: Stable, independent, self-aware → L3i: Integrated master clinician 🔍 **Discrimination Model** (Bernard): 3 roles × 3 areas = 9 possible focus points → Roles: Teacher, Counselor, Consultant → Areas: Process, Conceptualization, Personalization ⚖️ **Competency-Based:** → Focus on observable competencies → Benchmarks for evaluation **Key ethics:** Supervisors are responsible for supervisees' clients. Informed consent for supervision required.

memoryAids.title: Psychotropic Drug Classes
memoryAids.content: **"SMELT BAM"** — Major psychotropic drug families: 💊 **S**SRIs = Fluoxetine (Prozac), Sertraline (Zoloft) — 1st-line depression/anxiety 💊 **M**AOIs = Phenelzine, Tranylcypromine — Last resort! Tyramine diet (no aged cheese/wine) 💊 **E**ffexor-type (SNRIs) = Venlafaxine, Duloxetine — Serotonin + norepinephrine 💊 **L**ithium = Gold standard for BIPOLAR (narrow therapeutic window! Monitor blood levels) 💊 **T**CAs = Amitriptyline, Imipramine — Older, more side effects, lethal in overdose 💊 **B**enzodiazepines = Lorazepam, Diazepam — Fast anxiety relief, addictive 💊 **A**ntipsychotics = Typical (haloperidol) vs Atypical (clozapine, risperidone) 💊 **M**ethylphenidate (Ritalin) = ADHD stimulant (paradoxical calming) **Key:** SSRIs safest in overdose, MAOIs most dietary restrictions, Lithium needs blood monitoring

memoryAids.title: Brain Imaging Techniques
memoryAids.content: **"CT MRI PET fMRI EEG"** — From structure to function: 📸 **STRUCTURAL** (what it looks like): → **CT** scan = X-ray slices (fast, cheap, good for lesions/bleeding) → **MRI** = Magnetic, detailed soft tissue (no radiation!) 🎬 **FUNCTIONAL** (what it's doing): → **PET** = Radioactive glucose → shows metabolic activity (which areas are working hard) → **fMRI** = Blood oxygen levels → real-time brain activity (best spatial + temporal) → **EEG** = Electrical activity from scalp (best temporal resolution — milliseconds) **Memory trick:** → CT = "Cat scan" = quick snapshot → PET = needs a radioactive "pet" tracer → fMRI = "f" for "functional" MRI → EEG = "Electrical" waves on the scalp

memoryAids.title: Endocrine System & Hormones
memoryAids.content: **"TOP ACHE"** — Key endocrine glands & hormones: 🧠 **T**hyroid = Thyroxine (T4) — metabolism, energy → Hypo = fatigue, weight gain, depression-like. Hyper = anxiety-like 🫘 **O**varies/Testes = Estrogen, testosterone — sexual development 🦴 **P**ituitary = "Master gland" — controls all others via releasing hormones → Growth hormone, ACTH, TSH, oxytocin (bonding!), vasopressin 🔺 **A**drenal = Cortisol (stress), adrenaline (fight-or-flight) → Cushing's = too much cortisol. Addison's = too little ⚗️ **C**erebral cortex = not endocrine, but regulates via hypothalamus 🔬 **H**ypothalamus = Links nervous system TO endocrine system 🫀 **E**pineal = Melatonin (sleep-wake cycle)

memoryAids.title: Learned Helplessness & Seligman
memoryAids.content: **Core cue: experienced controllability changes later responding, but the theory has evolved.** Early animal studies found that prior uncontrollable aversive events could interfere with later escape learning. The original account proposed learning that outcomes were independent of responses. Later neuroscience reframed important mechanisms: passivity can be a default response to prolonged aversive stimulation, while detecting control recruits circuitry that can inhibit stress responses and support later resilience. The reformulated human model examined causal explanations using internal-external, stable-unstable, and global-specific dimensions, but "internal + stable + global = depression" is not a diagnosis or inevitable outcome. Avoid anthropomorphic dog stories, victim-blaming, and direct one-step generalization from laboratory stress to a person's depressive disorder.

sourceDetails.title: Learned helplessness at fifty: Insights from neuroscience
sourceDetails.organization: Steven F. Maier and Martin E. P. Seligman, Psychological Review
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A peer-reviewed fifty-year reappraisal by the original investigators that explicitly revises the early learning account using later evidence.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Updated the historical model, separated controllability research from diagnosis, and removed deterministic depressive attributions.

memoryAids.title: Language Development Theories
memoryAids.content: **"Skinner vs. Chomsky = Nature vs. Nurture of Language"** 🐀 **Skinner** (Behaviorist): Language learned through reinforcement → Parents shape babbling into words via attention/praise → Imitation + reinforcement = language acquisition 🧬 **Chomsky** (Nativist): Language is INNATE → **LAD** (Language Acquisition Device) — built-in grammar mechanism → **Universal Grammar** — all languages share deep structure → Evidence: Children make errors they've never heard ("I goed") 🤝 **Interactionist** (Vygotsky/Bruner): Both biology AND social interaction → LASS (Language Acquisition Support System) → Caregiver speech, scaffolding, social context **Critical period** (Lenneberg): Language easiest before puberty

memoryAids.title: Emotion Theories Compared
memoryAids.content: **"JC SL" — Four emotion theories in order:** 😨→💓→😱 **J**ames-Lange: Event → Body reacts FIRST → You label the emotion → "I'm trembling, therefore I must be afraid" → Problem: What about quadriplegics who still feel emotion? 😨💓😱 **C**annon-Bard: Event → Body AND emotion happen SIMULTANEOUSLY → Thalamus sends signals to cortex AND body at same time 💓+🏷️=😱 **S**chachter-Singer (Two-Factor): Arousal + Cognitive LABEL = Emotion → Bridge experiment: arousal + attractive person = attraction → Misattribution of arousal 🧠→😱 **L**azarus: Cognitive APPRAISAL comes FIRST → You must evaluate the situation before feeling emotion → Primary appraisal (threat?) → Secondary appraisal (can I cope?)

memoryAids.title: Signal Detection Theory
memoryAids.content: **Like a radiologist reading an X-ray — 4 possible outcomes:** | | Signal Present | Signal Absent | |--|--|--| | **Says YES** | ✅ HIT | ❌ FALSE ALARM | | **Says NO** | ❌ MISS | ✅ CORRECT REJECTION | **Two key factors:** → **Sensitivity (d')** = Ability to distinguish signal from noise → **Response bias (β)** = Tendency to say "yes" vs "no" **Liberal criterion** = Say yes a lot → more hits BUT more false alarms **Conservative criterion** = Say no a lot → fewer false alarms BUT more misses **Key:** Unlike classical threshold theory, SDT accounts for MOTIVATION and EXPECTATIONS, not just sensory ability

memoryAids.title: Weber's & Fechner's Laws
memoryAids.content: **Weber's Law** — The "percentage rule" of noticing change: → The JND (just noticeable difference) is a CONSTANT PROPORTION → If you're holding 100 lbs, you need ~2 lbs more to notice (2%) → If you're holding 10 lbs, you need ~0.2 lbs to notice (still 2%) **Formula:** ΔI/I = k (constant) **Fechner's Law** — Extends Weber: → As stimulus intensity increases, perceived change gets SMALLER → Like volume: going from 1→2 seems big, 99→100 seems small → S = k log(I) — sensation is proportional to LOG of intensity **Stevens' Power Law** — More modern, flexible: → Different exponents for different senses → Pain grows FASTER than proportional (accelerating)

memoryAids.title: Levels of Processing
memoryAids.content: **Craik & Lockhart** — Think of encoding like painting a fence: 🏷️ **Structural** (shallowest): "Is the word in UPPERCASE?" (appearance) → Weakest memory trace 🔊 **Phonemic** (middle): "Does the word rhyme with TRAIN?" (sound) → Better, but still surface-level 💡 **Semantic** (deepest): "Does the word fit in this sentence?" (meaning) → STRONGEST memory trace! **Key findings:** → **Self-reference effect** = Even deeper! "Does this apply to ME?" → **Elaborative rehearsal** > maintenance rehearsal → **Depth** of processing matters more than TIME spent studying **EPPP application:** Understanding concepts > rote memorization

memoryAids.title: Social Facilitation vs. Social Loafing
memoryAids.content: **Others watching you? It depends on the task!** 🏃 **Social Facilitation** (Zajonc): → Others' presence IMPROVES performance on **easy/well-learned** tasks → Others' presence IMPAIRS performance on **difficult/novel** tasks → Why? Arousal increases dominant response 😴 **Social Loafing** (Latané): → Individual effort DECREASES in groups → "Someone else will pick up the slack" → Worse with larger groups, anonymous contributions → Reduced by: making individual contributions identifiable **Key distinction:** → Facilitation = being EVALUATED → try harder → Loafing = NOT being evaluated → slack off

memoryAids.title: Groupthink
memoryAids.content: **Janis (1972) — "GRIPE"** symptoms of groupthink: 🤐 **G**uards against dissent (mind guards, self-censorship) 🛡️ **R**ationalization of warnings 🏰 **I**nvulnerability illusion ("We can't fail!") 📢 **P**ressure on dissenters ("Get with the program!") 🤝 **E**stimation of unanimity ("Everyone agrees!") Also: Stereotyping outgroups, illusion of morality **Prevention:** → Assign a devil's advocate → Invite outside experts → Leader withholds opinion initially → Anonymous input channels **Classic examples:** Bay of Pigs, Challenger disaster

memoryAids.title: Persuasion: ELM & Foot-in-Door
memoryAids.content: **Elaboration Likelihood Model (Petty & Cacioppo):** 🧠 **Central route** = Thoughtful analysis of ARGUMENT quality → When motivated + able to process → More durable attitude change 🎭 **Peripheral route** = Influenced by CUES (attractiveness, celebrity, emotion) → When NOT motivated or unable to process → Less durable, more susceptible to change **Compliance techniques:** 🚪 **Foot-in-Door** = Small request → then big request (Freedman & Fraser) → Consistency principle 🚪 **Door-in-Face** = BIG request (refused) → then small request (accepted) → Reciprocal concession 🏷️ **Low-balling** = Get commitment → then reveal hidden costs 🤝 **That's-not-all** = Offer extras before person decides

memoryAids.title: Self-Serving Bias & Just World
memoryAids.content: **Common attribution biases — "Everyone's the hero of their own story":** ⭐ **Self-serving bias** = → Success = "I'm talented!" (internal) → Failure = "The test was unfair!" (external) → Protects self-esteem ⚖️ **Just-world hypothesis** (Lerner): → "People get what they deserve" → Leads to VICTIM BLAMING ("They must have done something wrong") → Reduces threat: "If I'm good, bad things won't happen to me" 🤝 **False consensus effect:** → "Everyone agrees with me" (overestimate how common our views are) 🌟 **Halo effect:** → One positive trait → assume ALL positive traits → Attractive people = assumed to be smart, kind, etc.

memoryAids.title: Bronfenbrenner's Ecological Model
memoryAids.content: **Think of a set of Russian nesting dolls 🪆 — layers surround the child:** 👶 **Microsystem** — Immediate environment (family, school, peers) → Direct, face-to-face interactions 🔗 **Mesosystem** — Connections BETWEEN microsystems → Parent-teacher conferences, family-church links 🏢 **Exosystem** — Indirect influences (parent's workplace, school board) → Child doesn't participate directly but is affected 🌐 **Macrosystem** — Culture, laws, values, economic systems → "The water the fish doesn't know it's swimming in" ⏳ **Chronosystem** — Changes over TIME → Divorce, technology shifts, historical events **Memory trick:** Micro → Meso → Exo → Macro → Chrono "**M**y **M**om's **E**x **M**oved to **C**hina" (smallest to largest)

memoryAids.title: Marcia's Identity Statuses
memoryAids.content: **2×2 grid — Crisis × Commitment:** | | Commitment YES | Commitment NO | |--|--|--| | **Crisis YES** | 🏆 **Achievement** | 🔍 **Moratorium** | | **Crisis NO** | 🔒 **Foreclosure** | 😶 **Diffusion** | 🏆 **Achievement** = Explored AND committed (best outcome) 🔍 **Moratorium** = Actively exploring, haven't committed yet (healthy!) 🔒 **Foreclosure** = Committed WITHOUT exploring ("I'm a doctor because Dad is") 😶 **Diffusion** = No exploration, no commitment (apathetic) **Key:** Crisis = exploration/questioning. Not necessarily bad! **Marcia extended Erikson's** Identity vs. Role Confusion stage Adolescents often cycle through statuses (not linear)

memoryAids.title: Gilligan's Ethics of Care
memoryAids.content: **Gilligan's critique of Kohlberg — "Different voice, not lesser voice"** ⚖️ **Kohlberg** = Based on JUSTICE (rights, rules, abstract principles) → Studied mostly males → male moral reasoning as norm 💕 **Gilligan** = Based on CARE (relationships, responsibility, compassion) → Women more likely to use care-based reasoning → Not inferior, just DIFFERENT **Gilligan's 3 stages:** 1. **Pre-conventional** = Self-interest (survival) 2. **Conventional** = Self-sacrifice for others ("good girl") 3. **Post-conventional** = Balance self-care AND care for others **Key EPPP point:** Gilligan showed Kohlberg's model had GENDER BIAS → Both justice and care are valid moral frameworks

memoryAids.title: Language Milestones
memoryAids.content: **Age-by-age language timeline:** 🗣️ **0-3 months**: Cooing (vowel sounds — "oooh", "ahhh") 🗣️ **4-6 months**: Babbling (consonant-vowel combos — "ba-ba", "da-da") 🗣️ **8-12 months**: First words! ("mama", "dada" with meaning) 🗣️ **12-18 months**: One-word stage (holophrastic — 1 word = whole sentence) 🗣️ **18-24 months**: Two-word stage (telegraphic — "want cookie") → **Vocabulary explosion** at ~18 months! 🗣️ **2-3 years**: Sentences, overregularization ("I goed", "foots") 🗣️ **5-6 years**: ~2,000 words, basic grammar mastered **Key terms:** → **Overextension** = "doggy" for ALL animals → **Underextension** = "doggy" ONLY for their dog → **Motherese/child-directed speech** = Higher pitch, simpler words, repetition

memoryAids.title: Teratogens & Prenatal Development
memoryAids.content: **Prenatal risk depends on exposure, dose, timing, route, susceptibility, co-exposures, and the outcome measured; do not memorize a mixed "FATAL CATS" list.** A teratogen is an exposure capable of disrupting prenatal development under particular conditions. Alcohol has no known safe amount or time during pregnancy and can affect development throughout pregnancy. Some medications, infections, nicotine products, illicit substances, toxic metals, radiation levels, fever, and poorly controlled health conditions can increase specific risks, but they are not interchangeable. Maternal age is a risk factor, not a teratogen; ordinary stress and medically indicated diagnostic imaging should not be equated with known high-risk exposures. Do not advise abruptly stopping prescribed medication: current individualized medical and prenatal guidance is required.

sourceDetails.title: About Alcohol Use During Pregnancy
sourceDetails.organization: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
sourceDetails.whyReputable: Current official U.S. public-health guidance on timing, uncertainty, and documented developmental risks of prenatal alcohol exposure.

sourceDetails.title: Preventing Birth Defects
sourceDetails.organization: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
sourceDetails.whyReputable: Current federal guidance distinguishing modifiable exposures, infections, medications, health conditions, and preventive care.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Separated teratogens from risk factors, removed stigmatizing labels, and added dose, timing, susceptibility, and medication-safety boundaries.

memoryAids.title: Aging & Cognitive Changes
memoryAids.content: **"Fluid Falls, Crystal Climbs"** — The aging brain: 📉 **Fluid intelligence** = DECLINES with age → Processing speed, novel problem-solving, working memory → Peaks in 20s, gradual decline after 📈 **Crystallized intelligence** = INCREASES until very late → Vocabulary, general knowledge, wisdom → Accumulated expertise and experience **Other age-related changes:** → **Processing speed** ↓ (biggest decline!) → **Source memory** ↓ ("I know this but where did I hear it?") → **Prospective memory** ↓ ("Remember to take pills at 2pm") → **Procedural memory** = relatively PRESERVED → **Recognition** holds up better than recall **NOT normal aging:** Forgetting how to do familiar tasks, personality changes = get evaluated!

memoryAids.title: Rorschach & TAT (Projective Tests)
memoryAids.content: **"Rorschach = inkblots, TAT = stories"** 🦋 **Rorschach Inkblot Test:** → 10 inkblot cards (5 B&W, 5 color) → "What might this be?" — scored by Exner's Comprehensive System → Scores: location, determinants, content, popularity → Controversy: Critics say poor reliability/validity 📖 **TAT** (Thematic Apperception Test — Murray): → Ambiguous pictures of people in situations → "Tell me a story about this picture" → Reveals needs, motives, conflicts through narrative → Based on Murray's need theory **Both are PROJECTIVE** — assume ambiguous stimuli reveal unconscious material **Contrast with OBJECTIVE tests** (MMPI) — structured, standardized, empirically keyed

memoryAids.title: ADHD Diagnostic Criteria
memoryAids.content: **ADHD — 3 presentations, onset before age 12:** 🏃 **Predominantly Inattentive:** → 6+ symptoms for 6+ months (5+ if age 17+) → Doesn't listen, loses things, easily distracted, forgetful → Often misdiagnosed in girls (less disruptive) 💥 **Predominantly Hyperactive-Impulsive:** → 6+ symptoms for 6+ months → Fidgets, runs/climbs, talks excessively, can't wait turn, interrupts 🔄 **Combined:** Both inattention AND hyperactive-impulsive **Key DSM-5 requirements:** → Onset before age **12** (was 7 in DSM-IV) → Symptoms in **2+ settings** (home, school, work) → Must impair functioning → Not better explained by another disorder **Treatment:** Stimulants (methylphenidate) = 1st line. Also behavior therapy.

memoryAids.title: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)
memoryAids.content: **DSM-5 ASD — 2 core domains (was 3 in DSM-IV):** 👥 **A. Social communication deficits** (all 3 required): → Social-emotional reciprocity problems → Nonverbal communication deficits → Difficulty with relationships 🔁 **B. Restricted, repetitive behaviors** (2 of 4): → Stereotyped movements/speech (echolalia, hand-flapping) → Insistence on sameness, rigid routines → Highly fixated interests (intense focus on trains, numbers) → Sensory hyper/hypo-reactivity **Key DSM-5 changes:** → SPECTRUM = no more separate Asperger's, PDD-NOS → Severity levels: 1 (needs support) → 3 (needs very substantial support) → Must be present in early development (but may not fully manifest until social demands exceed capacity)

memoryAids.title: OCD Diagnostic Features
memoryAids.content: **OCD = Obsessions + Compulsions (or both):** 🧠 **Obsessions** = Intrusive, unwanted, recurring THOUGHTS → Contamination fears, harm fears, symmetry needs, taboo thoughts → Cause marked anxiety/distress → Person TRIES to suppress them (ego-dystonic) ✋ **Compulsions** = Repetitive BEHAVIORS or mental acts → Handwashing, checking, counting, ordering, praying → Performed to REDUCE anxiety from obsessions → Excessive or not realistically connected to threat **Key distinctions:** → OCD is NOT in the Anxiety Disorders chapter in DSM-5! → Moved to "Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders" → Related: Body Dysmorphic, Hoarding, Trichotillomania, Excoriation **Best treatment:** ERP (Exposure and Response Prevention) + SSRI

memoryAids.title: Bipolar Disorder Types
memoryAids.content: **"Bipolar I = 1 manic episode. Bipolar II = 2nd-tier mania (hypomania)"** 🔴 **Bipolar I:** → At LEAST 1 **manic** episode (7+ days or hospitalization) → Mania: grandiosity, decreased sleep, pressured speech, racing thoughts, risky behavior → May also have depressive episodes (but NOT required!) 🟡 **Bipolar II:** → At least 1 **hypomanic** episode (4+ days) + 1 **major depressive** episode → Hypomania = less severe, no psychosis, no hospitalization → NOT a "milder" form — depression can be severe! 🔄 **Cyclothymic Disorder:** → Chronic (2+ years) fluctuating mood → Hypomanic + depressive symptoms (not full episodes) **Key:** Bipolar I = manic episode REQUIRED. Bipolar II = NO full mania ever (if full mania → reclassify as Bipolar I)

memoryAids.title: Intellectual Disability Criteria
memoryAids.content: **3 criteria ALL required (DSM-5):** 1. 🧠 **Intellectual deficits** — IQ ~70 or below (but NOT just a number!) → Clinical assessment + standardized testing 2. 🏠 **Adaptive functioning deficits** — 1+ domains: → Conceptual (academics, reasoning) → Social (empathy, communication, judgment) → Practical (self-care, job, money management) 3. ⏰ **Onset during developmental period** (before age 18) **Severity levels** (based on ADAPTIVE functioning, not IQ!): → Mild (85%): IQ ~55-70, can live independently with support → Moderate: IQ ~40-55, some independent living → Severe: IQ ~25-40, needs significant support → Profound: IQ <25, full-time care **DSM-5 change:** Severity now based on adaptive function, NOT just IQ score!

memoryAids.title: Neuropsychological Tests
memoryAids.content: **"HB WCC"** — Key neuropsych tests for the EPPP: 🧠 **H**alstead-Reitan = Comprehensive battery (wide-ranging) → Category test, finger tapping, trail making → Impairment Index (0 = normal, 1 = impaired) 🧠 **B**oston Naming = Confrontation naming (shows pictures) → Good for aphasia, dementia screening 🧠 **W**AIS-IV subtests = Many double as neuropsych screens → Digit Span = attention/working memory → Block Design = visuospatial 🧠 **C**lock Drawing Test = Quick screen for dementia → "Draw a clock showing 10 after 11" → Tests planning, spatial, executive function 🧠 **Trail Making A & B:** → A = connecting numbers (speed) → B = alternating numbers & letters (executive function/switching) → B > A difference = executive dysfunction

memoryAids.title: Exposure & Response Prevention (ERP)
memoryAids.content: **ERP is collaborative exposure plus response prevention for OCD.** Build a shared formulation and hierarchy; approach external or internal triggers in a planned, safe, consent-based way; and refrain from or reduce the linked compulsion, ritual, avoidance, or reassurance/safety behavior long enough to learn something new. Distress may rise, fall, fluctuate, or remain present. Benefit is not limited to "anxiety must habituate"; inhibitory learning and expectancy violation emphasize learning that feared outcomes are less certain or more manageable than predicted. Tailor pacing, accessibility, risk assessment, developmental context, and support; response prevention is not coercive blocking.

sourceDetails.title: Obsessive-compulsive disorder and body dysmorphic disorder: treatment (CG31)
sourceDetails.organization: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A public clinical guideline developed through systematic evidence review and transparent recommendation methods.

sourceDetails.title: Maximizing exposure therapy: An inhibitory learning approach
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A peer-reviewed clinical-science review explaining why exposure learning should not be reduced to within-session habituation.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Replaced coercive blocking and mandatory habituation with collaborative, inhibitory-learning-aware ERP.

memoryAids.title: EMDR — Eye Movement Desensitization
memoryAids.content: **Shapiro (1989) — EMDR for trauma/PTSD:** **8 phases:** 1. History & treatment planning 2. Preparation (explain procedure, teach coping) 3. Assessment (identify target memory, negative cognition, SUD) 4. **Desensitization** (bilateral stimulation + trauma memory) 5. Installation (strengthen positive cognition) 6. Body scan (check for residual tension) 7. Closure (return to equilibrium) 8. Re-evaluation (next session) **Bilateral stimulation** = eye movements, tapping, or tones → Proposed: mimics REM sleep processing → Controversy: Is it the eye movements or just the exposure? **Evidence:** → Effective for PTSD (APA conditionally recommended) → Critics: exposure component may be the active ingredient → Eye movements may be unnecessary (dismantling studies)

memoryAids.title: Behavioral Activation
memoryAids.content: **For depression: "Action BEFORE motivation"** Depression cycle: 😞 Feel bad → 🛋️ Withdraw → 😞 Feel worse → 🛋️ Withdraw more **Behavioral Activation breaks the cycle:** → Schedule ACTIVITIES even when you don't feel like it → Start with small, manageable, pleasurable activities → Activity → natural reinforcement → improved mood → Monitor mood BEFORE and AFTER activities **Based on:** Lewinsohn's behavioral model of depression → Depression = low rate of RESPONSE-CONTINGENT reinforcement → Treatment = increase positive reinforcement in daily life **EPPP key:** BA has strong evidence as stand-alone treatment → Component of CBT but also works independently

memoryAids.title: Paradoxical Intention
memoryAids.content: **"Prescribe the symptom!" — Used in different therapies:** 🔄 **Viktor Frankl** (Logotherapy/Existential): → Tell the anxious person to TRY to have anxiety → The effort to produce the symptom interrupts it → Removes anticipatory anxiety 🎯 **Strategic Family Therapy** (Haley, Madanes): → Prescribe the problematic behavior → If family follows directive → therapist has control → If family resists directive → problem behavior stops! → Either way, the therapist wins **Example:** → Insomnia patient: "Try to stay awake as long as possible" → Fighting couple: "Argue for exactly 30 minutes at 7pm daily" **Key:** Works by disrupting the cycle of TRYING to suppress symptoms

memoryAids.title: Evidence-Based Treatment Pairings
memoryAids.content: **Evidence-based practice is not a one-disorder/one-brand lookup table.** Integrate the best available research with clinical expertise and the person's characteristics, culture, preferences, goals, access needs, comorbidity, risk, setting, and prior response. Exam pairings can identify well-supported options: exposure-based methods for many anxiety presentations, ERP for OCD, behavioral activation or structured psychotherapies for depression, and trauma-focused psychotherapies for PTSD. Medication, combined care, family or systems work, and level of care may also be indicated. "Most effective," "gold standard," and "CBT works for everything" require a defined population, comparator, outcome, time point, harms, and evidence source. Shared decision-making, competence, monitoring, and revising a nonbeneficial plan remain essential.

sourceDetails.title: Evidence-based practice
sourceDetails.organization: APA Dictionary of Psychology, American Psychological Association
sourceDetails.whyReputable: APA's professional definition explicitly integrates research, clinical expertise, culture, preferences, and patient characteristics.

sourceDetails.title: Clinical Practice Guideline for the Treatment of PTSD in Adults
sourceDetails.whyReputable: An APA guideline based on systematic evidence review that models bounded recommendations, comparisons, patient factors, and harms.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Replaced a branded treatment lookup list with evidence integration, shared decisions, defined claims, and monitoring.

memoryAids.title: Play Therapy
memoryAids.content: **"Play is the language of children"** — Virginia Axline: 🧸 **Directive Play Therapy:** → Therapist structures activities, guides themes → Uses specific techniques (sand tray, puppets, art) → More common in CBT approaches for kids 🎨 **Non-Directive Play Therapy** (Axline): → Child leads, therapist follows → Based on Rogers' person-centered approach → Axline's 8 principles include: accepting the child, reflecting feelings, following the child's lead → The therapist provides a safe space; the child's play IS the communication **Key techniques:** → **Sand tray** — Miniature figures in sand (both Jungian and directive) → **Filial therapy** — Train PARENTS to do play therapy → **Puppet play** — Child projects feelings onto puppets **Recommended age:** 3-12 years

memoryAids.title: Reliability Types
memoryAids.content: **"TISI"** — The 4 types of reliability: 🔁 **T**est-Retest: Same test, same people, two time points → Measures STABILITY over time → Concern: Practice effects from repeat testing 📋 **I**nter-Rater (Inter-Observer): Two raters score the same responses → Measures AGREEMENT between scorers → Critical for projective tests, behavioral observation ✂️ **S**plit-Half: Divide test in half, compare scores → Measures INTERNAL CONSISTENCY → Spearman-Brown correction adjusts for shorter test length 📊 **I**nternal Consistency (Cronbach's α): How well items correlate with each other → Alpha ≥ .70 = acceptable → Alpha ≥ .80 = good → Measures homogeneity of items **Key:** ALL forms establish consistency. Only VALIDITY tells you if you're measuring the RIGHT thing.

memoryAids.title: Validity Types
memoryAids.content: **"CCC + E"** — The 4 types of validity: 📝 **C**ontent Validity: Does the test cover the full DOMAIN? → Does the EPPP test all 8 knowledge areas? → Based on EXPERT JUDGMENT (not statistics) 🎯 **C**riterion Validity: Does the test predict or correlate with a CRITERION? → **Predictive** = GRE predicts grad school GPA → **Concurrent** = New depression scale correlates with Beck's 🧩 **C**onstruct Validity: Does the test measure the theoretical CONSTRUCT? → **Convergent** = Correlates with similar measures ✅ → **Discriminant** = Does NOT correlate with unrelated measures ❌ → Factor analysis supports construct validity 🏠 **E**cological Validity: Does it apply to real-world settings? → Lab results ≠ real life behavior

memoryAids.title: Sampling Methods
memoryAids.content: **"SCCS"** — Probability sampling methods: 🎲 **S**imple Random: Every person has EQUAL chance (gold standard) → Like drawing names from a hat 📊 **S**tratified: Divide population into SUBGROUPS, then random sample from each → Ensures representation (e.g., equal gender proportions) → Best when you need demographic representation 🏘️ **C**luster: Randomly select GROUPS, then test everyone in group → Ex: Randomly pick 10 schools, test ALL students in those schools → Easier/cheaper but more sampling error 📏 **S**ystematic: Every Nth person (e.g., every 5th chart) **Non-probability sampling:** → **Convenience** = whoever is available (weakest) → **Snowball** = participants recruit more participants → **Purposive** = researcher picks who fits

memoryAids.title: Factor Analysis
memoryAids.content: **Factor analysis = Finding hidden themes in a pile of data:** Imagine 50 test items. Factor analysis reveals: items 1-10 cluster together, 11-20 cluster... Those clusters ARE the factors. 📊 **Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA):** → "What factors exist?" — Discovers structure → Used when building a new test → No prior hypothesis about # of factors 📊 **Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA):** → "Do MY proposed factors fit the data?" — Tests a model → Used to validate a theory → Requires prior hypothesis **Factor loading** = correlation between item and factor → Loading ≥ .30 = acceptable, ≥ .50 = strong **Eigenvalue** = amount of variance explained by factor → Eigenvalue > 1.0 usually retained **EPPP key:** Construct validity evidence!

memoryAids.title: Chi-Square Test
memoryAids.content: **Chi-Square = The COUNTING test 📊** When your data is CATEGORIES (nominal), not numbers: → "Is there a relationship between gender and major?" → "Do therapy outcomes differ from expected rates?" **Two types:** 🔲 **Goodness-of-Fit** (one variable): → Does observed distribution match expected? → Ex: Are equal numbers choosing each option? 📊 **Test of Independence** (two variables): → Are the variables related? → Ex: Is treatment type related to recovery (yes/no)? **Assumptions:** → Data is FREQUENCY (counts, not scores) → Expected frequency ≥ 5 per cell → Observations are independent **If expected < 5 → use Fisher's Exact Test** **Non-parametric** — no normality assumption needed

memoryAids.title: Statistical Power
memoryAids.content: **Power is conditional: P(reject H0 | a specified alternative, design, and analysis).** It equals 1 - beta for that specified alternative. Power changes with sample size, effect magnitude, outcome variability/reliability, design efficiency, allocation, alpha, multiplicity, missing data, and the statistical model. Increasing alpha can increase power while increasing Type I error; it is not a free improvement. ".80" is a planning convention, not a universal quality threshold or an 80% chance that a discovered effect is real. Plan with a smallest effect of interest and sensitivity analysis. Post hoc "observed power" calculated from the observed effect is largely redundant with the p value and does not rescue an inconclusive study.

sourceDetails.title: A power primer
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A peer-reviewed methodological primer that explains power as conditional on effect size, alpha, sample size, and design assumptions.

sourceDetails.title: The ASA Statement on p-Values: Context, Process, and Purpose
sourceDetails.whyReputable: An expert consensus statement warning against binary and post hoc interpretations of statistical evidence.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Corrected the probability statement, bounded .80, and removed observed-power and real-effect interpretations.

memoryAids.title: Meta-Analysis Concepts
memoryAids.content: **Meta-analysis = "A study of studies"** 📊 **What it does:** → Statistically combines results from MULTIPLE studies → Calculates overall effect size across studies → Highest level of evidence! **Key concepts:** → **Inclusion criteria** = Which studies qualify (peer-reviewed? RCT?) → **Effect size** = Common metric across studies (Cohen's d, r) → **Homogeneity test** = Are study results consistent? → **Forest plot** = Visual display of individual + combined effects → **Funnel plot** = Checks for publication bias **Publication bias** (the "file drawer problem"): → Significant results more likely to be published → Non-significant results sit in file drawers → Meta-analysis may overestimate effect if biased toward published studies **Famous example:** Smith & Glass (1977) — therapy effective (d = 0.68)

memoryAids.title: Competence Boundaries
memoryAids.content: **APA Standard 2.01 — "Stay in your lane"** 🎓 **You may ONLY practice within your:** → Education → Training → Supervised experience → Consultation/study/professional experience **What to do when faced with unfamiliar territory:** 1. REFER to someone competent 2. Get SUPERVISION or consultation 3. Obtain additional TRAINING 4. Use the literature **Emerging areas** (Standard 2.01e): → Can practice with reasonable steps to ensure competence → When no one else is available (rural, emergency) **Key EPPP scenario:** → Client presents with culture you don't know → CONSULT, don't just refer → New technique you haven't trained in → Get supervised, don't wing it → Emergency: Act even if outside competence → then refer ASAP

memoryAids.title: Fees, Bartering & Pro Bono
memoryAids.content: **APA Standard 6.04-6.07 — Money matters:** 💰 **Fees:** → Discuss fees EARLY in treatment → Cannot withhold records for nonpayment if emergency → Don't charge for missed sessions unless clearly agreed in advance 🔄 **Bartering** (Standard 6.05): → Not inherently unethical! → OK if: (1) not clinically contraindicated, (2) not exploitative → Example OK: Trading therapy for car repair in rural area → Example NOT OK: Patient does personal favors creating power imbalance 💝 **Pro Bono:** → APA ENCOURAGES (not requires) some pro bono work → Sliding scales should be considered for those who can't pay **Key:** NEVER abandon a client because they can't pay! → Arrange alternative services, pro bono, or appropriate referral

memoryAids.title: Forensic Psychology Ethics
memoryAids.content: **Forensic = Legal system + Psychology** ⚖️ **Key differences from clinical work:** → Client may be the COURT, not the examinee → Confidentiality is LIMITED (report goes to court) → Informed consent must clarify WHO gets the report → Must explain limits before the evaluation 📋 **Competency evaluations:** → Competency to stand trial (CST) = understands charges + can assist attorney → Not the same as insanity defense (MSO at time of crime) → Legal standard, not clinical diagnosis 🔍 **Insanity defense (M'Naghten):** → At time of crime, didn't know nature of act OR didn't know it was wrong → NGRI (Not Guilty by Reason of Insanity) → Very rare — used in <1% of cases **Key:** Forensic psychologists must be objective and unbiased, even if retained by one side

memoryAids.title: Child Custody Evaluation
memoryAids.content: **"Best Interests of the Child" = Primary standard** 📋 **APA Guidelines for Child Custody:** → Focus on CHILD'S needs, not parents' desires → Use multiple methods of data collection → Interview BOTH parents and the child → Use psychological testing when appropriate → Stay OBJECTIVE — don't become an advocate for either parent 🚫 **Common pitfalls:** → DON'T see only one parent and give opinion → DON'T use projective tests as sole basis → DON'T make custody recommendations outside your competence → DON'T serve as both therapist AND evaluator (dual role!) **Key terms:** → **Joint custody** = shared legal/physical custody → **Guardian ad litem** = appointed to represent child's interests → **Parental alienation** = controversial concept — use carefully

memoryAids.title: Multicultural Ethics & Guidelines
memoryAids.content: **APA Multicultural Guidelines (2017) — "Beyond Sue's AKS":** 🌍 **Key principles:** 1. Psychologists aspire to recognize how IDENTITY shapes experiences 2. Aspire to recognize and understand that POWER, PRIVILEGE, and oppression → psychological outcomes 3. Aspire to understand how THEIR OWN cultural background influences their work 💡 **ADDRESSING model** (Hays): → **A**ge/generation → **D**evelopmental/acquired disabilities → **R**eligion/spirituality → **E**thnic/racial identity → **S**ocioeconomic status → **S**exual orientation → **I**ndigenous heritage → **N**ational origin → **G**ender **Key EPPP point:** Not knowing a client's culture ≠ incompetence. Not SEEKING to learn about it = incompetence.

memoryAids.title: Dissociative Disorders
memoryAids.content: **"DAD" — The 3 dissociative disorders:** 🪞 **D**issociative Identity Disorder (DID): → 2+ distinct personality states ("alters") → Gaps in recall of everyday events, personal info, trauma → NOT the same as schizophrenia! (common misconception) → Associated with severe childhood trauma/abuse → Controversial: Some argue it's iatrogenic (therapy-induced) 😶 **D**issociative **A**mnesia: → Unable to recall important autobiographical info (usually traumatic) → Too extensive for normal forgetting → May include **dissociative fugue** (travel + identity confusion) 🌫️ **D**epersonalization/Derealization Disorder: → Depersonalization = feeling detached from SELF ("watching myself") → Derealization = feeling world is UNREAL ("everything looks fake") → Reality testing remains INTACT

memoryAids.title: Somatic Symptom vs Factitious vs Malingering
memoryAids.content: **"SFC" — Three look-alikes, very different motivations:** 😰 **Somatic Symptom Disorder:** → REAL distress about somatic symptoms → Excessive thoughts, feelings, behaviors about health → Symptoms may or may not be medically explained → NOT faking! Genuine anxiety about health → DSM-5 replaced hypochondriasis & somatization 🎭 **Factitious Disorder** (Munchausen): → FAKING/inducing illness for the "sick role" → No external reward — just wants to BE a patient → **By proxy** (imposed on another) = caregiver makes child sick → Internal motivation 🤥 **Malingering:** → FAKING symptoms for EXTERNAL gain (money, avoid jail, disability) → NOT a mental disorder — it's intentional deception → External motivation **Key:** Somatic = genuine distress. Factitious = fake for sick role. Malingering = fake for profit.

memoryAids.title: Eating Disorders
memoryAids.content: **"ABB" — The Big 3 eating disorders:** 🍽️ **A**norexia Nervosa: → Restriction of intake → significantly low body weight → Intense FEAR of gaining weight → Disturbed body image (sees self as fat despite being thin) → Types: Restricting vs Binge-Purge → Highest mortality of ANY mental disorder! 🔄 **B**ulimia Nervosa: → Binge eating + compensatory behavior (purging, exercise, fasting) → At least 1×/week for 3 months → Self-evaluation unduly influenced by body shape/weight → Usually NORMAL weight (key difference from anorexia) 🍔 **B**inge Eating Disorder: → Binge episodes WITHOUT purging/compensation → Marked distress about binging → At least 1×/week for 3 months → Often associated with obesity → NEW in DSM-5 (was in appendix of DSM-IV) **Tx:** CBT-E for bulimia/BED, FBT (Maudsley) for adolescent anorexia

memoryAids.title: Substance Use Disorder Criteria
memoryAids.content: **DSM-5: 2+ of 11 criteria in 12 months:** **"WITHDRAWAL HITS"** — The 11 criteria grouped: 💊 **Impaired Control (4):** → Using more/longer than intended → Can't cut down despite wanting to → Excessive time getting/using/recovering → Craving 🏠 **Social Impairment (3):** → Failure to fulfill role obligations → Continued use despite social problems → Activities given up or reduced ⚠️ **Risky Use (2):** → Use in hazardous situations → Continued despite knowing physical/psychological harm 🧪 **Pharmacological (2):** → **Tolerance** (need more for same effect) → **Withdrawal** (symptoms when stopping) **Severity:** Mild (2-3), Moderate (4-5), Severe (6+) **DSM-5 change:** Combined DSM-IV "abuse" and "dependence" into ONE spectrum

memoryAids.title: Sleep-Wake Disorders
memoryAids.content: **Common sleep disorders on the EPPP:** 😴 **Insomnia Disorder:** → Difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep, 3+ nights/week, 3+ months → Most common sleep disorder → CBT-I = first-line treatment (NOT medication!) 💤 **Narcolepsy:** → Irresistible sleep attacks → Cataplexy = sudden muscle weakness triggered by emotion → Hypnagogic hallucinations (falling asleep), sleep paralysis → Due to loss of hypocretin/orexin neurons 🌙 **Sleep Apnea (Obstructive):** → Breathing stops repeatedly during sleep → Excessive daytime sleepiness, loud snoring → Treatment: CPAP machine 🚶 **Parasomnias:** → Sleepwalking & night terrors = during NREM (deep sleep) → Nightmare disorder = during REM → REM Sleep Behavior Disorder = act out dreams (no paralysis) **Key:** Night terrors ≠ nightmares! Terrors = NREM, no recall. Nightmares = REM, vivid recall.

memoryAids.title: Conversion Disorder (Functional Neurological)
memoryAids.content: **The body "converts" psychological stress into physical symptoms:** 🧠→🦵 **What it looks like:** → Neurological symptoms WITHOUT neurological cause → Paralysis, blindness, seizures, numbness, movement problems → Symptoms don't match known neurological patterns → "La belle indifference" = surprising lack of concern (classic but not universal) **Key distinctions:** → NOT faking (unlike malingering/factitious) → Genuine distress or functional impairment → Previously called "hysteria" (Freud/Charcot) → Often follows psychological stressor **DSM-5 change:** → No longer requires identified psychological stressor → Emphasis on POSITIVE neurological findings of incompatibility (symptoms inconsistent with recognized neurological conditions) → Renamed "Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder"

memoryAids.title: Negative Reinforcement vs. Punishment
memoryAids.content: **Classify a consequence with two questions:** Was a stimulus added (+) or removed (?)? Did the future probability of behavior increase (reinforcement) or decrease (punishment)? Positive reinforcement adds and increases; negative reinforcement removes/avoids and increases; positive punishment adds and decreases; negative punishment removes and decreases. Positive/negative do not mean good/bad, and the label depends on observed behavior change?not intention. Escape and avoidance are common negative-reinforcement arrangements; response cost is one form of negative punishment.

sourceDetails.title: Psychology 2e, Section 6.3: Operant Conditioning
sourceDetails.whyReputable: An expert-authored, peer-reviewed open textbook from nonprofit Rice University.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Made classification functional and corrected the time-out/response-cost equivalence.

memoryAids.title: Extinction & Spontaneous Recovery
memoryAids.content: **Extinction = Stop reinforcing → behavior DIES (slowly, with drama!)** 📉 **Extinction in operant conditioning:** → Stop reinforcing a previously reinforced behavior → **Extinction burst** = behavior temporarily INCREASES/gets worse! (Like pressing an elevator button harder when it doesn't work) → Then gradually decreases 📉 **Extinction in classical conditioning:** → Present CS without UCS repeatedly → CR weakens and eventually stops 🔄 **Spontaneous recovery:** → After extinction, the behavior/CR can REAPPEAR after a rest period → Usually weaker than original → Shows extinction ≠ unlearning (it's NEW learning that inhibits the old) **Key EPPP fact:** Intermittent reinforcement → HARDEST to extinguish (VR schedule = most resistant to extinction)

memoryAids.title: Classical Conditioning Key Terms
memoryAids.content: **"The Pavlov Toolkit" — Beyond the basics:** 🔔 **Stimulus Generalization** = Similar stimuli → similar response → Dog salivates to bells AND buzzers → Little Albert feared rats AND rabbits/fur coats (Watson) 🔕 **Stimulus Discrimination** = Respond to CS but NOT similar stimuli → Dog salivates to 1000 Hz tone, not 500 Hz 🔗 **Higher-Order Conditioning** = CS1 paired with CS2 (no UCS needed) → Bell → food (1st order). Light → bell (2nd order). Light now triggers salivation! 😨 **Conditioned Emotional Response** = Fear/emotion via conditioning → Little Albert: rat (CS) + loud noise (UCS) → fear of rats (CR) 🍽️ **Taste Aversion** (Garcia Effect): → One-trial learning! (violates contiguity principle) → Get sick after eating → avoid that food FOREVER → Evolutionary advantage: don't eat poisonous things twice

memoryAids.title: Stress & Coping Models
memoryAids.content: **Lazarus & Folkman's Transactional Model:** 🧠 **Primary Appraisal:** "Is this a threat?" → Irrelevant, benign-positive, or STRESSFUL (harm/threat/challenge) 🧠 **Secondary Appraisal:** "Can I cope with this?" → What resources do I have? What can I do? **Two coping styles:** 🔧 **Problem-focused** = Fix the problem directly → Study harder for the test, talk to your boss → Best when you CAN control the situation 💭 **Emotion-focused** = Manage the feelings → Venting, denial, reframing, seeking support → Best when you CAN'T control the situation **Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS):** → **Alarm** → **Resistance** → **Exhaustion** → Chronic stress → exhaustion → illness/burnout **Key:** Coping style should MATCH the controllability of the stressor

memoryAids.title: Stereotype Threat
memoryAids.content: **Claude Steele — "The weight of negative stereotypes"** 📉 When you KNOW a negative stereotype about your group exists... → Anxiety about confirming it → impairs performance → You don't even have to BELIEVE the stereotype! **Classic studies:** → Black students told test measured INTELLIGENCE → scored lower → Same test framed as "problem-solving exercise" → no gap! → Women told math test showed gender differences → scored lower → White men told test compared them to ASIANS → scored lower! **Reduces performance through:** → Anxiety, divided attention, extra self-monitoring → Working memory overload (worrying uses cognitive resources) **Reduce stereotype threat by:** → Emphasize growth mindset → Reframe the test ("not measuring ability") → Role models from same group → Self-affirmation exercises
