memoryAids.title: Suicide Risk Assessment
memoryAids.content: **Do not use SAD PERSONS or any demographic checklist as a prediction rule. Use a direct, dynamic safety assessment.** Ask about current and recent suicidal thoughts, frequency, plan, method, access to means, intent, past attempts/self-injury, symptoms and substance use, acute stressors, supports, and reasons for living. Past suicidal behavior is important, but no single factor determines disposition. Current suicidal thoughts, feasible plans/access, intent, inability to maintain safety, rapid change, and clinical context require timely escalation under setting and jurisdiction procedures. Collaboratively create a concrete safety plan and address lethal-means access; a "no-suicide contract" does not establish safety. If danger may be imminent, use emergency resources and do not leave the person unsupported.

sourceDetails.title: Adult Outpatient Brief Suicide Safety Assessment Guide
sourceDetails.organization: National Institute of Mental Health, National Institutes of Health
sourceDetails.whyReputable: An official federal clinician guide covering current thoughts, plans, access, past behavior, symptoms, supports, safety planning, and disposition.

sourceDetails.title: Risk and Protective Factors for Suicide
sourceDetails.organization: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
sourceDetails.whyReputable: Official U.S. public-health guidance emphasizing interacting individual, relationship, community, and societal factors rather than one demographic score.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed a poorly predictive, stereotype-prone score and replaced it with NIMH's direct multifactorial assessment structure.

memoryAids.title: Crisis Intervention
memoryAids.content: **Crisis care is person-centered, safety-oriented, least restrictive, accessible, and connected to follow-up; it is not a fixed ABC script or session count.** Establish engagement and communication access, clarify the person's view and immediate needs, assess medical and behavioral urgency, suicide or violence risk, substance effects, supports, environment, and access to lethal means, then collaborate on the safest feasible next steps. Use existing strengths and natural supports with consent, address practical needs, create a concrete safety and follow-up plan, coordinate handoffs, and document reasoning. Do not promise confidentiality beyond its limits, use "normal reaction" as a substitute for assessment, or assume returning to pre-crisis functioning is every person's goal. Emergency and jurisdiction procedures govern imminent danger.

sourceDetails.title: 2025 National Guidelines for a Behavioral Health Coordinated System of Crisis Care
sourceDetails.organization: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration
sourceDetails.whyReputable: Current federal crisis-system guidance emphasizing person-centered, accessible, coordinated, least restrictive, and follow-up care.

sourceDetails.title: Adult Outpatient Brief Suicide Safety Assessment Guide
sourceDetails.organization: National Institute of Mental Health, National Institutes of Health
sourceDetails.whyReputable: An official federal clinician guide for direct safety assessment, supports, plans, access to means, and disposition decisions.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed a fixed script and duration, added direct safety assessment, accessibility, least-restrictive care, handoffs, and follow-up.

memoryAids.title: Leadership Theories
memoryAids.content: **"TCT SL" — Major leadership theories:** 👤 **T**rait Theory: Leaders are BORN, not made → Traits: intelligence, self-confidence, integrity, sociability → Weakness: ignores situation 📋 **C**ontingency/Situational Theories: → **Fiedler's Contingency**: Leader style is FIXED; match leader to situation → Task-oriented leaders best in HIGH or LOW control → Relationship-oriented leaders best in MODERATE control → **Hersey & Blanchard's Situational**: Adapt style to follower maturity 🔄 **T**ransformational vs Transactional (Burns/Bass): → **Transactional** = Exchange rewards for performance (if-then) → **Transformational** = Inspire, challenge, intellectual stimulation → 4 I's: Idealized influence, Inspirational motivation, Intellectual stimulation, Individualized consideration 🤝 **S**ervant **L**eadership: Leader serves followers first

memoryAids.title: Performance Appraisal Errors
memoryAids.content: **"CHLRS" — 5 rater biases to know:** 🌟 **C**entral Tendency: Rate everyone in the MIDDLE → Avoids extremes, plays it safe ✨ **H**alo Effect: One good trait → rate ALL traits high → (Horn effect = opposite: one bad → all bad) 🎁 **L**eniency/Severity: Rate everyone too HIGH (lenient) or too LOW (severe) 🕐 **R**ecency Effect: Rate based on RECENT performance only → Ignores performance over the whole period 📊 **S**imilar-to-Me: Rate people like yourself more favorably **Remedies:** → BARS (Behaviorally Anchored Rating Scale) = reduces bias → 360-degree feedback = multiple rater perspectives → BOS (Behavioral Observation Scale) = frequency of behaviors → Forced distribution = prevents central tendency

memoryAids.title: Job Satisfaction & Motivation Theories
memoryAids.content: **Workplace motivation — Key theories:** 💼 **Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory:** → **Hygiene factors** (dissatisfiers): Salary, conditions, supervision, policy → Having them prevents UNhappiness, but doesn't create happiness → **Motivators** (satisfiers): Achievement, recognition, growth, responsibility → These create actual job SATISFACTION 📊 **Equity Theory** (Adams): → Compare your input/output ratio to others' → Underpaid = distress, anger. Overpaid = guilt → Restore equity by: changing effort, changing perception, leaving 🎯 **Expectancy Theory** (Vroom): → Motivation = Expectancy × Instrumentality × Valence → "Can I do it?" × "Will it lead to reward?" × "Do I value the reward?" 😁 **Hawthorne Effect:** → Performance improves simply because people know they're being OBSERVED → Western Electric studies (Mayo, 1920s-30s)

memoryAids.title: Prenatal Development Stages
memoryAids.content: **"GEF" — Three stages of prenatal development:** 🔬 **G**erminal (0-2 weeks): → Conception → implantation → Zygote → blastocyst → Implants in uterine wall (~day 10-14) 🫀 **E**mbryonic (2-8 weeks): → **MOST CRITICAL PERIOD** for teratogens! → All major organs and structures forming → Heart, brain, limbs, eyes developing → Most vulnerable to birth defects 👶 **F**etal (8 weeks - birth): → Growth, maturation, refinement → "Age of viability" = ~22-26 weeks (can survive outside womb) → Brain develops rapidly in 3rd trimester → Myelination begins **Key EPPP points:** → Cephalocaudal (head→down) & Proximodistal (center→out) principles → Most structural damage from teratogens = embryonic period → Brain damage possible throughout pregnancy

memoryAids.title: Piaget's Key Concepts Deep Dive
memoryAids.content: **Beyond the 4 stages — Piaget's mechanisms:** 🧩 **Schema** = Mental framework for understanding the world 🔄 **Adaptation** has TWO processes: → **Assimilation** = Fit new info INTO existing schema (Child sees a horse: "Look, a big doggy!") → **Accommodation** = CHANGE schema to fit new info (Child learns: "That's a horse, not a dog") ⚖️ **Equilibration** = Balance between assimilation & accommodation → Disequilibrium = confusion when schema doesn't work → motivates learning **Key concepts by stage:** → **Object permanence** = Sensorimotor (tested with A-not-B error) → **Egocentrism** = Preoperational (three mountains task) → **Conservation** = Concrete Operational (liquid, mass, number) → **Abstract thinking** = Formal Operational (pendulum problem) **Criticism:** Piaget UNDERESTIMATED children's abilities

memoryAids.title: Mandated Reporting Details
memoryAids.content: **Mandated reporting is a current-law lookup, not "when in doubt, report."** Determine the jurisdiction, protected population, whether the psychologist's role is covered, reporting threshold, source and timing rules, designated agency, required oral or written follow-up, privilege provisions, and emergency procedures. Child, elder, dependent-adult, intimate-partner, and other reporting duties are not interchangeable. Many child-abuse statutes use reasonable suspicion or reasonable cause rather than proof, but wording and deadlines vary, and not every state names every profession in the same way. Explain confidentiality limits early and, when safe and legally appropriate, tell the client what will be disclosed. Report only what the law requires or permits, consult promptly when uncertain, protect privacy, document the authority and facts, and never let consultation create a prohibited delay.

sourceDetails.title: Mandatory Reporting of Child Abuse and Neglect
sourceDetails.organization: Children's Bureau, Administration for Children and Families, U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
sourceDetails.whyReputable: An official federal state-statutes synthesis that expressly documents variation in covered reporters, standards, and reporting provisions.

sourceDetails.title: Ethical Principles of Psychologists and Code of Conduct
sourceDetails.whyReputable: APA's official ethics text for confidentiality limits, legally mandated or permitted disclosures, minimizing intrusion, and documentation context.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Replaced a universal report-first slogan with a jurisdiction, role, threshold, timing, privacy, and documentation decision structure.

memoryAids.title: Test Security & Release
memoryAids.content: **Distinguish test data from test materials, then identify authority and risk.** Test data include scores, client responses, and examination notes. Under APA Standard 9.04, psychologists generally provide data pursuant to a client/patient release, with a narrow option to refrain for likely substantial harm or substantial misuse/misrepresentation; absent release, provide only as required by law or court order. Test materials include manuals, instruments, protocols, and questions/stimuli; Standard 9.11 calls for reasonable security efforts consistent with law and contract. Avoid blanket never-release rules.

sourceDetails.title: Ethical Principles of Psychologists and Code of Conduct
sourceDetails.whyReputable: APA?s official primary text for the General Principles and enforceable Ethical Standards.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Corrected blanket unqualified-recipient and never-release rules.

memoryAids.title: TBI & Neurological Conditions
memoryAids.content: **Key neurological concepts for the EPPP:** 🧠 **Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI):** → Mild (concussion) = brief LOC, GCS 13-15 → Moderate/Severe = extended LOC, GCS ≤ 12 → Post-concussion syndrome: headaches, memory, irritability → **Coup-contrecoup** = damage at impact site AND opposite side ⚡ **Seizures/Epilepsy:** → **Generalized** (whole brain): Tonic-clonic (grand mal), absence (petit mal) → **Focal/Partial**: One area. Simple (conscious) vs Complex (altered consciousness) → Treatment: Anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, valproate) 🧪 **Dementia types:** → **Alzheimer's** = Gradual onset, plaques + tangles, memory → executive → **Vascular** = Stepwise decline, following strokes → **Frontotemporal (Pick's)** = Personality/behavior changes first → **Lewy Body** = Visual hallucinations + motor symptoms **Key:** Depression can mimic dementia ("pseudodementia") — ALWAYS assess!

memoryAids.title: Test Bias & Fairness
memoryAids.content: **Group score differences, DIF, bias, and fairness are related but not interchangeable.** A mean difference alone does not establish measurement bias. **DIF** flags an item when matched examinees from groups have different response probabilities; it triggers content and construct review but is not automatically proof of bias. Fairness also includes access, standardized and accessible administration, appropriate accommodations, construct-relevant content, valid interpretation across intended groups, opportunity to demonstrate the construct, and responsible consequences/use. Differential prediction and validity require appropriate models and evidence. No instrument is culture-free, and one court ruling or "culture-fair" label does not settle a new use.

sourceDetails.title: Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing (2014)
sourceDetails.organization: American Educational Research Association, American Psychological Association, and National Council on Measurement in Education
sourceDetails.whyReputable: The joint professional consensus framework for score interpretation, fairness, validity, precision, and responsible test use.

sourceDetails.title: NAEP Technical Documentation: Differential Item Functioning
sourceDetails.whyReputable: Official federal technical documentation that explicitly distinguishes a statistical DIF flag from a determination of item bias.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Separated DIF from bias, expanded fairness beyond prediction, and removed universal legal and culture-free claims.

memoryAids.title: Interpersonal Therapy (IPT)
memoryAids.content: **IPT = Focus on RELATIONSHIPS as the path to symptom relief:** **Developed by Klerman & Weissman for depression** 📋 **4 problem areas** (pick 1-2 to focus on): 😢 **Grief** = Complicated bereavement → Help process the loss, build new relationships ⚔️ **Role disputes** = Conflicts with significant others → Communication analysis, negotiation skills 🔄 **Role transitions** = Life changes (divorce, retirement, new job) → Mourning old role, embracing new role 👤 **Interpersonal deficits** = Social isolation, poor social skills → Build communication skills, reduce isolation **Key facts:** → Time-limited (12-16 sessions typically) → Evidence-based for depression (comparable to CBT) → Focus on HERE-AND-NOW relationships → NOT about changing personality — about improving communication

memoryAids.title: Criterion-Referenced vs. Norm-Referenced Tests
memoryAids.content: **These terms describe score interpretations, not mutually exclusive kinds of tests.** A criterion-referenced interpretation compares performance with a defined domain, performance standard, or cut score. A norm-referenced interpretation locates performance in a specified reference-population distribution. The same assessment can support both only when evidence justifies each use. A cut score is a decision boundary established through a standard-setting process, not an error-free "absolute" measure of competence; a percentile is meaningful only for its named norm group and date. ASPPB currently recommends 500 as the passing score for EPPP Part 1-Knowledge, but the licensing jurisdiction determines applicable requirements. Do not generalize that number to every EPPP component, historical scale, jurisdiction, or future integrated examination.

sourceDetails.title: NCME Assessment Glossary
sourceDetails.organization: National Council on Measurement in Education
sourceDetails.whyReputable: Field-standard definitions distinguishing criterion-referenced and norm-referenced interpretations, cut scores, norms, and validation.

sourceDetails.title: Examination for Professional Practice in Psychology (EPPP)
sourceDetails.organization: Association of State and Provincial Psychology Boards
sourceDetails.whyReputable: The examination owner's current official page for recommended passing scores, exam components, and jurisdiction-dependent requirements.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Corrected the test-type dichotomy, bounded cut-score meaning, and made the current EPPP recommendation jurisdiction- and version-specific.

memoryAids.title: Scales of Measurement
memoryAids.content: **"NOIR" — From weakest to strongest:** 🏷️ **N**ominal = Names/categories ONLY → Gender, diagnosis, eye color → Can only count frequencies (mode) → Chi-square test 📊 **O**rdinal = Ranked ORDER, but unequal intervals → "1st, 2nd, 3rd" — Gold, Silver, Bronze → Class rank, Likert scales (debatable) → Median, Spearman rho 📐 **I**nterval = Equal intervals, NO true zero → Temperature (°F, °C), IQ scores, calendar year → 20°C is NOT "twice as warm" as 10°C → Mean, Pearson r, t-test, ANOVA 📏 **R**atio = Equal intervals + TRUE ZERO → Height, weight, reaction time, income → "Twice as heavy" IS meaningful → All statistics apply **Key EPPP tip:** Most psychological variables are interval or quasi-interval

memoryAids.title: Gender Identity Development
memoryAids.content: **Key concepts — terminology matters on the EPPP:** 🧬 **Sex** = Biological (chromosomes, hormones, anatomy) 👤 **Gender identity** = Internal sense of being male, female, non-binary, etc. 🎭 **Gender expression** = How you present (clothing, behavior, mannerisms) 💕 **Sexual orientation** = Who you're attracted to (separate from gender!) **Developmental milestones:** → **2-3 years**: Gender identity established ("I'm a boy/girl") → **4-5 years**: Gender stability ("I'll always be a boy/girl") → **6-7 years**: Gender constancy/consistency (Kohlberg) → Understand gender doesn't change with appearance **Gender Dysphoria (DSM-5):** → Marked incongruence between experienced gender and assigned sex → Duration: 6+ months → Causes clinically significant distress → Affirming care = evidence-based approach

memoryAids.title: HIPAA Essentials for Psychologists
memoryAids.content: **HIPAA = Health Insurance Portability & Accountability Act (1996)** 🔒 **Privacy Rule** — Who can see your records: → PHI (Protected Health Information) = any individually identifiable health info → "Minimum necessary" standard: share ONLY what's needed → Client has RIGHT to access their records → Client can request amendments to records 🔐 **Security Rule** — Protecting electronic records: → Administrative safeguards (training, policies) → Physical safeguards (locks, screen position) → Technical safeguards (encryption, passwords) 📋 **Key exceptions** (no authorization needed): → Treatment, payment, healthcare operations (TPO) → Required by law (court orders, mandated reporting) → Public health/safety threats → Abuse/neglect reporting **Psychotherapy notes** = EXTRA protected! → Separate from medical record → Requires SPECIFIC authorization to release (even to insurance!) → Cannot be require as condition of treatment

memoryAids.title: Action Potential Steps
memoryAids.content: **Threshold ? rising phase ? falling phase ? afterhyperpolarization ? recovery.** At threshold, voltage-gated sodium conductance rises; sodium-channel inactivation and delayed potassium conductance then drive repolarization. Continued potassium conductance can briefly make the membrane more negative than rest. The absolute refractory period reflects unavailable sodium channels; during the relative refractory period another spike needs stronger input. Myelin permits saltatory propagation between excitable nodes. Exact voltages and timing vary?memorize the mechanism, not one universal number.

sourceDetails.title: Biology 2e, Section 35.2: How Neurons Communicate
sourceDetails.whyReputable: An expert-authored, peer-reviewed open textbook from nonprofit Rice University.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Corrected phase names, refractory mechanisms, and universal voltage claims.

memoryAids.title: Neuron Structure
memoryAids.content: **A neuron is like a tree:** 🌿 **Dendrites** = Branches (RECEIVE signals) → More dendrites = more connections = more learning 🪵 **Cell body (Soma)** = Trunk (PROCESSES info) → Contains nucleus, keeps cell alive → Summation happens here (excitatory + inhibitory) 🏗️ **Axon** = Root going underground (SENDS signals) → One long fiber, covered in myelin sheath → Carries action potential away from cell body 🔌 **Axon terminals** = Root tips (TRANSMIT to next neuron) → Contain synaptic vesicles with neurotransmitters → Release into synaptic cleft **Synapse** = Gap between two neurons → Neurotransmitters cross this gap → Lock-and-key: NT binds to specific receptor **Direction:** Dendrite → Soma → Axon → Terminal (always one-way!)

memoryAids.title: Glial Cells
memoryAids.content: **Glia support, regulate, defend, and myelinate?do not reduce them to passive ?glue.?** Astrocytes help regulate the extracellular environment, metabolism, synapses, and blood?brain-barrier functions. Oligodendrocytes form CNS myelin and can support segments on multiple axons; Schwann cells myelinate PNS segments and support repair. Microglia are resident immune-surveillance and response cells. Ependymal cells line ventricles and participate in cerebrospinal-fluid physiology. Cell counts and roles vary by region; avoid the outdated ?glia outnumber neurons 10:1 everywhere? shortcut.

sourceDetails.title: Neuroscience, 2nd edition: ?Glial Cells?
sourceDetails.organization: NCBI Bookshelf, U.S. National Library of Medicine
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A scholarly neuroscience textbook hosted by the U.S. National Library of Medicine.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed passive-glue and universal 10:1 claims.

memoryAids.title: Gestalt Principles of Perception
memoryAids.content: **"COPS-FC" — 6 Gestalt grouping principles:** 🔄 **C**losure = Brain fills in gaps to see complete shapes → You see a circle even if part is missing 🔲 **F**igure-Ground = Separate object from background → Vase/faces illusion 📏 **P**roximity = Group things that are NEAR each other → ● ● ● ● ● ● = you see two groups, not six dots ↔️ **S**imilarity = Group things that LOOK alike → ●○●○ vs ●●○○ = different groupings 〰️ **C**ontinuity = Follow smooth, continuous lines → See flowing lines, not jagged angles 🎯 **C**ommon fate = Things moving TOGETHER = same group → Flock of birds moving in formation **Gestalt motto:** "The whole is GREATER than the sum of its parts"

memoryAids.title: Depth Perception Cues
memoryAids.content: **Monocular vs Binocular — How we see in 3D:** 👁️ **Monocular cues** (one eye): → **Relative size** — smaller = farther away → **Interposition/overlap** — blocking = in front → **Linear perspective** — lines converge at distance → **Texture gradient** — detail decreases with distance → **Motion parallax** — close things move faster past you → **Accommodation** — lens changes shape for focus 👀 **Binocular cues** (two eyes): → **Convergence** — eyes turn inward for close objects → **Retinal disparity** — each eye gets slightly different image → Greater disparity = CLOSER object **Gibson vs Gregory:** → Gibson = Direct perception (info is in the stimulus) → Gregory = Constructive (brain interprets/constructs) **Key:** Binocular cues only work for CLOSE objects (~25 feet)

memoryAids.title: Attention Models
memoryAids.content: **"BBC" — 3 classic attention models:** 📻 **B**roadbent = Early Selection (Filter Theory) → Filter happens EARLY based on physical features (pitch, location) → Unattended info is completely blocked → Problem: Cocktail party effect (you DO hear your name!) 🔍 **T**reisman = Attenuation Theory → Unattended info is WEAKENED (turned down), not blocked → Important info breaks through (like your name) → Better explains cocktail party effect 📋 **D**eutsch & Deutsch = Late Selection → ALL info is processed for meaning → Filter happens LATE at response stage → Most demanding on cognitive resources **Key EPPP fact:** Selective attention = focusing on one thing Divided attention = multitasking (limited capacity!) Inattentional blindness = missing obvious things when focused (gorilla study)

memoryAids.title: Problem-Solving Barriers
memoryAids.content: **"FAME" — 4 barriers to effective problem-solving:** 🔧 **F**unctional fixedness = Can only see object's TYPICAL use → Duncker's candle problem: can't see box as a shelf → Can't think outside the box (literally!) 🧠 **A**nchoring = First piece of info dominates thinking → Higher starting price → higher final estimate → Hard to adjust away from initial anchor 🗺️ **M**ental set = Using strategies that worked BEFORE → Einstellung effect: stuck in old approach → "If all you have is a hammer, everything looks like a nail" 💡 **E**xpertise blindness = Experts overlook simple solutions → Overthinking what a novice would solve easily **Problem-solving strategies:** → **Algorithm** = Try every possibility (slow but guaranteed) → **Heuristic** = Rule of thumb (fast but error-prone) → **Insight** = Sudden "aha!" moment (Köhler's apes + bananas)

memoryAids.title: Types of Conformity
memoryAids.content: **Kelman's 3 levels — from shallow to deep:** 🎭 **Compliance** = Go along publicly, disagree privately → "I'll say what you want to hear" → Least lasting, most superficial → Normative influence (want to be liked) 🤝 **Identification** = Conform because you VALUE the group/person → "I want to be like THEM" → Moderate depth, lasts while relationship does → Example: copying a mentor's style 💎 **Internalization** = Genuinely ADOPT the belief → "I truly believe this now" → Deepest, most permanent change → Informational influence (they must be right) **Two types of social influence:** → **Normative** = Want to fit in → compliance → **Informational** = Want to be correct → internalization **Asch = normative** (people KNEW the right answer) **Sherif (autokinetic) = informational** (ambiguous situation)

memoryAids.title: Aggression Theories
memoryAids.content: **5 perspectives on WHY we aggress:** 🧬 **Biological** = Innate drive → Lorenz: instinctual (hydraulic model) → Testosterone, amygdala, serotonin (low = more aggression) 😤 **Frustration-Aggression** (Dollard) = Frustration ALWAYS leads to aggression → Revised (Berkowitz): Frustration → anger → aggression only IF cues present → Weapons effect: presence of guns increases aggression 👀 **Social Learning** (Bandura) = Learned by observation → Bobo doll: watch aggression → imitate aggression → Reinforcement: rewarded aggression → more aggression 🌡️ **Excitation Transfer** (Zillmann): → Residual arousal from one situation → misattributed to next → Exercise → argument → EXTRA angry (leftover arousal) 🎮 **Cognitive Neoassociation** (Berkowitz): → Negative affect → activates aggression-related thoughts → Hostile cues in environment trigger aggressive schemas

memoryAids.title: Attitude Formation & Change
memoryAids.content: **"ABC" of attitudes + how they change:** 🧠 **A**ffect = How you FEEL about it (emotions) 🚶 **B**ehavior = What you DO about it (actions) 💭 **C**ognition = What you THINK about it (beliefs) **Attitude change theories:** 🔄 **Cognitive Dissonance** (Festinger): → Behavior ≠ belief → discomfort → change attitude → Less external justification = MORE attitude change 📊 **Self-Perception Theory** (Bem): → "I must like it because I keep doing it" → Infer attitudes from observing own behavior → Works for WEAK or ambiguous attitudes 🧲 **Balance Theory** (Heider): → We want consistency in our P-O-X triads → Imbalanced = uncomfortable → change attitude **Key EPPP fact:** Cognitive dissonance predicts change for STRONG attitudes Self-perception theory predicts change for WEAK attitudes

memoryAids.title: Bowlby's Attachment Phases
memoryAids.content: **4 phases of attachment development:** 👶 **Phase 1** (0-2 months): **Pre-attachment** → Indiscriminate social responsiveness → Orients to anyone, no specific preference 😊 **Phase 2** (2-7 months): **Attachment-in-the-making** → Discriminating sociability → Prefers familiar people but no protest at separation 😢 **Phase 3** (7-24 months): **Clear-cut attachment** → Separation anxiety + stranger anxiety emerge! → Secure base behavior (explore, return) → Peak separation anxiety: 12-18 months 🤝 **Phase 4** (24+ months): **Goal-corrected partnership** → Understands caregiver's goals/plans → Can negotiate separations **Internal Working Model (IWM):** → Mental representation of relationships → Guides expectations in ALL future relationships → Secure IWM: "I'm worthy, others are trustworthy"

memoryAids.title: Theory of Mind & Sally-Anne
memoryAids.content: **Theory of Mind = Understanding that others have DIFFERENT thoughts than you** 🧠 Develops around age **4** (range: 3-5) **Sally-Anne Test (Baron-Cohen):** → Sally puts marble in basket, leaves room → Anne MOVES marble to box → Sally returns. Where will Sally look? → Correct (age 4+): Sally looks in BASKET (where SHE left it) → Incorrect (age 3): Sally looks in BOX (where it ACTUALLY is) **Key connections:** → **False belief understanding** = hallmark of ToM → **Autism:** Often delayed or impaired ToM (Baron-Cohen's research) → **Egocentrism** (Piaget): Preoperational children struggle with ToM → **Machiavellian intelligence**: Using ToM to manipulate **"Mind-blindness"** (Baron-Cohen) = Difficulty inferring others' mental states → Core feature in autism spectrum disorder

memoryAids.title: Moral Development — Full Comparison
memoryAids.content: **Piaget → Kohlberg → Gilligan → Turiel — Moral reasoning evolution:** 👶 **Piaget** (2 stages): → Heteronomous (5-7): Rules are FIXED, judge by OUTCOME → Autonomous (10+): Rules are flexible, judge by INTENTION 📊 **Kohlberg** (3 levels, 6 stages): → Pre-conventional: Self-interest (punishment, reward) → Conventional: Society (approval, law & order) → Post-conventional: Principles (social contract, universal ethics) → Used HEINZ DILEMMA (steal drug for dying wife?) 💕 **Gilligan** (3 stages): → Self-interest → Self-sacrifice → Balance of self & others → Ethics of CARE, not just justice → Critiqued Kohlberg's male bias 🏫 **Turiel** — Domain Theory: → MORAL (harm, fairness) vs CONVENTIONAL (etiquette, rules) vs PERSONAL (preference) → Even young children distinguish these domains!

memoryAids.title: IQ Score Interpretation
memoryAids.content: **Wechsler IQ — What the numbers mean:** 📊 **Mean = 100, SD = 15** | Score Range | Classification | Percentile | |---|---|---| | 130+ | Very Superior | 98th | | 120-129 | Superior | 91st-97th | | 110-119 | High Average | 75th-90th | | 90-109 | Average | 25th-74th | | 80-89 | Low Average | 9th-24th | | 70-79 | Borderline | 2nd-8th | | <70 | Extremely Low | <2nd | **Quick SD math:** → 1 SD above = 115 = 84th percentile → 2 SDs above = 130 = 98th percentile → 1 SD below = 85 = 16th percentile → 2 SDs below = 70 = 2nd percentile **68-95-99.7 rule:** → 68% within ±1 SD (85-115) → 95% within ±2 SD (70-130) → 99.7% within ±3 SD (55-145)

memoryAids.title: DSM-5 Anxiety vs OCD vs Trauma
memoryAids.content: **DSM-5 SPLIT these into 3 SEPARATE chapters:** 😰 **Anxiety Disorders:** → GAD, Panic, Social Anxiety, Specific Phobia → Separation Anxiety, Selective Mutism, Agoraphobia → Core feature: FEAR/ANXIETY about future threat 🔁 **Obsessive-Compulsive & Related:** → OCD, Body Dysmorphic, Hoarding → Trichotillomania (hair pulling), Excoriation (skin picking) → Core feature: REPETITIVE behaviors/thoughts 💥 **Trauma & Stressor-Related:** → PTSD, Acute Stress, Adjustment Disorders → Reactive Attachment, Disinhibited Social Engagement → Core feature: EXPOSURE to traumatic/stressful event **Why it matters for EPPP:** → DSM-IV had ONE big anxiety chapter → DSM-5 split based on different underlying mechanisms → OCD is NOT an anxiety disorder anymore! → PTSD is NOT an anxiety disorder anymore!

memoryAids.title: Object Relations Therapy
memoryAids.content: **"KWM" — 3 key object relations theorists:** 🧸 **K**lein = Paranoid-Schizoid & Depressive Positions → Splitting: infant sees objects as ALL good or ALL bad → Paranoid-schizoid: fear of persecution, splitting → Depressive: recognizing whole objects (good + bad) → Projective identification: put your feelings INTO others 🧷 **W**innicott = Good Enough Mother & Transitional Objects → Perfect mothering is NOT needed — just "good enough" → Transitional object: teddy bear bridges self & other → True self vs False self (compliance mask) → Holding environment: safe therapeutic space 🔗 **M**ahler = Separation-Individuation → Normal Autism (0-1 mo) → Symbiosis (1-5 mo) → Separation-Individuation: Differentiation → Practicing → Rapprochement → Object Constancy → Rapprochement crisis (~18 mo): "Go away! Come back!" **Key:** All emphasize early relationships shape adult personality & pathology

memoryAids.title: ACT — Acceptance & Commitment Therapy
memoryAids.content: **"HEXAFLEX" — ACT's 6 core processes:** 🧘 **Acceptance** = Open up to difficult feelings (don't fight them) → Opposite of experiential avoidance 👁️ **Defusion** = Step back from thoughts ("I notice I'm having the thought that...") → Thoughts are just words, not facts 🕐 **Present Moment** = Contact the here and now → Mindful awareness of current experience 🪞 **Self-as-Context** = You are NOT your thoughts → Observing self vs thinking self → "I am the sky; thoughts are the weather" 🧭 **Values** = What matters MOST to you? → Chosen life directions (not goals — ongoing) → "If you could be remembered for anything..." 🚶 **Committed Action** = Take steps aligned with values → Even when it's hard or scary → Behavior change anchored in meaning **Goal:** Psychological FLEXIBILITY (not symptom elimination!) **Hayes (founder):** Change relationship with thoughts, not the thoughts themselves

memoryAids.title: Correlation Pitfalls
memoryAids.content: **"DROIT" — 5 things that affect correlation coefficients:** 📏 **D**irection — Positive (+) or Negative (−) → +r: both increase together → −r: one increases, other decreases → Strong ≠ positive! r = −0.90 is STRONGER than r = +0.40 📐 **R**ange restriction — Truncated range REDUCES r → Studying GPA-intelligence only in PhD students → low r → Because the range of intelligence is restricted 🔵 **O**utliers — Can dramatically inflate OR deflate r → One extreme point can create a "false" correlation 📊 **I**nterpretation — Correlation ≠ causation! → Third variable problem: Z causes both X and Y → Directionality problem: Does X→Y or Y→X? 🔄 **T**ype — Different types for different data: → Pearson r = interval/ratio data → Spearman rho = ordinal data → Point-biserial = one dichotomous + one continuous → Phi coefficient = both dichotomous

memoryAids.title: Normal Distribution Properties
memoryAids.content: **The Bell Curve — Everything you need to know:** 📊 **68-95-99.7 Rule:** → 68% within ±1 SD of mean → 95% within ±2 SD → 99.7% within ±3 SD 📐 **Properties:** → Mean = Median = Mode (all at center) → Symmetrical around the mean → Tails extend to infinity (asymptotic) → Total area under curve = 100% 📈 **Skewness:** → Positive skew: tail to RIGHT, mean PULLED RIGHT → Negative skew: tail to LEFT, mean PULLED LEFT → "The tail tells the tale" — skew named for tail direction → Order: Mean is always closest to the tail 📏 **Kurtosis:** → Leptokurtic = Tall & narrow ("lepto = lean") → Platykurtic = Flat & wide ("platy = platypus = flat") → Mesokurtic = Normal curve **Key:** With skewed data, MEDIAN is better than mean

memoryAids.title: APA Ethical Standards vs Principles
memoryAids.content: **APA Code:** General Principles A?E are aspirational ideals; Ethical Standards contain potentially enforceable rules. A: Beneficence and Nonmaleficence; B: Fidelity and Responsibility; C: Integrity; D: Justice; E: Respect for People?s Rights and Dignity. Principles still guide reasoning when rules leave judgment. APA ethics findings, licensing-board actions, civil liability, employment discipline, and criminal law are different processes; an alleged Standard violation does not automatically mean loss of license. Identify the authority, current provision, jurisdiction, and facts.

sourceDetails.title: Ethical Principles of Psychologists and Code of Conduct
sourceDetails.whyReputable: APA?s official primary text for the General Principles and enforceable Ethical Standards.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Corrected automatic-sanction language and distinguished governing authorities.

memoryAids.title: Privilege & Exceptions
memoryAids.content: **Psychotherapist-Patient Privilege — "JAFFEE":** ⚖️ **Jaffee v. Redmond (1996):** → US Supreme Court recognized psychotherapy privilege FEDERALLY → Communications in therapy are protected from disclosure in court → Applies to psychologists, psychiatrists, AND social workers 🔓 **Privilege can be WAIVED by:** → The CLIENT (not the therapist!) → Privilege belongs to the client 🚫 **Privilege does NOT apply when:** → Court-ordered evaluation (no privilege if not true "therapy") → Client introduces mental health as issue in litigation → Therapist is defendant (malpractice suit) → Child custody evaluations (varies by jurisdiction) → Danger to self/others (Tarasoff overrides privilege) **Key distinction:** → **Confidentiality** = ethical duty (APA) → **Privilege** = legal protection (courts) → Privilege is NARROWER than confidentiality → A court can compel testimony even if it violates confidentiality

memoryAids.title: Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome
memoryAids.content: **"ARE" — 3 stages of stress response:** 🚨 **A**larm = Body detects threat → fight-or-flight → HPA axis activates, cortisol + adrenaline surge → Heart rate ↑, blood pressure ↑, digestion ↓ → Resources mobilized for survival 💪 **R**esistance = Body adapts to ongoing stress → Cortisol stays elevated but body copes → Looks functional on outside but resources draining → Immune system begins to weaken 😵 **E**xhaustion = Body's resources DEPLETED → Immune collapse, organ damage, illness → Burnout, depression, cardiovascular disease → "The body keeps the score" **Key EPPP connections:** → Psychoneuroimmunology (PNI): stress → immune suppression → Type A personality → higher cardiac risk (Friedman & Rosenman) → Allostatic load = cumulative wear from chronic stress

memoryAids.title: Circadian Rhythms & Sleep Disorders
memoryAids.content: **"SCM" — Your body's 3 built-in clocks:** 🌞 **S**uprachiasmatic Nucleus (SCN) = Master clock → Located in hypothalamus → Receives light input from retina → Controls ~24-hour cycle 🌙 **Melatonin** = Darkness hormone → Released by pineal gland → Makes you sleepy at night → Suppressed by blue light (screens!) ☀️ **Cortisol** = Awakening hormone → Peaks in morning (cortisol awakening response) → Helps you wake up and stay alert **Disruptions:** → Jet lag: SCN out of sync with new time zone → Shift work: fighting your natural rhythm → SAD (Seasonal Affective Disorder): less daylight → depression → Treatment: Light therapy, melatonin supplements **Sleep architecture changes with age:** → Elderly: Less deep sleep (Stage 3), more awakenings → Infants: ~50% REM (adults ~20-25%)

memoryAids.title: Consciousness & Altered States
memoryAids.content: **Levels and altered states of consciousness:** 🧠 **Consciousness continuum:** → Alert → Daydreaming → Meditation → Hypnosis → Sleep → Coma 💤 **Hypnosis (Hilgard):** → Neodissociation theory: divided consciousness → "Hidden observer" = part of mind remains aware → Increased suggestibility, focused attention → NOT sleep! EEG shows waking patterns → Does NOT create false memories (but increases confidence in them!) 🧘 **Meditation:** → Mindfulness = present-moment, non-judgmental awareness → Concentration = focus on single object (mantra, breath) → Benefits: reduced cortisol, increased gray matter, lower anxiety 🍄 **Psychoactive drugs** (by class): → Depressants: alcohol, benzos, barbiturates (↓ CNS activity) → Stimulants: caffeine, cocaine, amphetamines (↑ CNS activity) → Hallucinogens: LSD, psilocybin (distort perception) → Opioids: morphine, heroin (pain relief, euphoria)

memoryAids.title: Piaget's Conservation Tasks
memoryAids.content: **Conservation = Understanding that quantity stays the same despite appearance change** 🧃 **Liquid conservation:** → Pour same water into tall thin vs short wide glass → Pre-op child: "The tall glass has MORE!" → Concrete op child: "Same amount, just different shape" 🪙 **Number conservation:** → Spread out a row of coins vs squish them together → Pre-op: "The spread-out row has MORE coins!" 🏔️ **Mass conservation:** → Flatten a ball of clay into a pancake → Pre-op: "The pancake has MORE clay!" **Why pre-op kids fail:** → **Centration** = Focus on ONE dimension (height only) → **Irreversibility** = Can't mentally "undo" the transformation → **Appearance over reality** = Looks different = IS different **Conservation is mastered in Concrete Operations (7-11)** → First: number & liquid (~6-7), Last: volume (~11-12)

memoryAids.title: Short-Term vs Working Memory
memoryAids.content: **Short-term memory emphasizes brief retention; working memory emphasizes temporary maintenance plus controlled use.** In Baddeley?s model, the phonological loop supports speech-based information, the visuospatial sketchpad supports visual/spatial information, the central executive coordinates attention, and the limited-capacity episodic buffer integrates information across systems and long-term memory. ?7 ? 2? came from particular span tasks and is not a universal working-memory capacity. Different models define working memory differently; name the model and task instead of treating one architecture as settled fact.

sourceDetails.title: The episodic buffer: a new component of working memory?
sourceDetails.organization: Alan Baddeley, Trends in Cognitive Sciences
sourceDetails.whyReputable: A peer-reviewed article by the originator of the influential multicomponent model.

memoryAids.reviewNote: Removed universal 7?2 and passive-versus-active claims; bounded the model.

memoryAids.title: Minority Influence
memoryAids.content: **Moscovici — How minorities change majority opinion:** 🔵 **Blue-Green Slide Study** (1969): → Confederates consistently called blue slides "green" → Majority began to shift! (8% said green in experimental condition) → But only when minority was CONSISTENT **4 keys to minority influence:** 💎 **C**onsistency = Say the same thing every time → Most important factor! → Both synchronic (everyone agrees) and diachronic (over time) 🤝 **C**ommitment = Show sacrifice and conviction → "They must really believe this if they're willing to suffer" 🔄 **F**lexibility = Be willing to negotiate (not rigid) → Rigid minority = dismissed; flexible = taken seriously 📊 **I**dentification = Ingroup minority more persuasive → "One of us" who disagrees is harder to dismiss **Key:** Minority influence → INFORMATIONAL influence → internalization Majority influence → NORMATIVE influence → compliance

memoryAids.title: Prosocial Behavior & Altruism
memoryAids.content: **Why do people help? 4 explanations:** 🧬 **Kin Selection** (Hamilton): → Help relatives = protect shared genes → More likely to help close relatives than strangers → Inclusive fitness: your genes survive through kin 🤝 **Reciprocal Altruism** (Trivers): → Help others expecting future help in return → "You scratch my back, I'll scratch yours" → Works in stable social groups 😊 **Empathy-Altruism** (Batson): → TRUE altruism exists when motivated by empathy → Empathic concern → help regardless of personal gain → Personal distress → help to REDUCE OWN discomfort 📊 **Negative-State Relief** (Cialdini): → Helping reduces YOUR negative mood → Selfish motivation disguised as altruism → "I help because it makes ME feel better" **Factors increasing helping:** → Good mood, guilt, seeing others help, rural setting → Similarity to victim, clear need, personal responsibility

memoryAids.title: Adult Attachment Styles
memoryAids.content: **Hazan & Shaver — Infant attachment → Adult romantic attachment:** | Infant (Ainsworth) | Adult (Hazan & Shaver) | |---|---| | Secure | Secure | | Avoidant | Dismissive-Avoidant | | Ambivalent | Anxious-Preoccupied | | Disorganized | Fearful-Avoidant | 🟢 **Secure** (~55%): Comfortable with intimacy & independence → "I find it easy to get close to others" 🔵 **Dismissive-Avoidant** (~25%): Self-reliant, avoids closeness → "I don't need close relationships" → High self-esteem, low view of others 🟡 **Anxious-Preoccupied** (~10%): Clingy, fear abandonment → "I want to be closer than my partner wants" → Low self-esteem, high view of others 🔴 **Fearful-Avoidant** (~10%): Want closeness BUT fear rejection → "I want it but I'm afraid of getting hurt" → Low self-esteem, low view of others **Bartholomew's 2×2:** Self-view (+ or −) × Other-view (+ or −)

memoryAids.title: Self-Concept Development
memoryAids.content: **How the sense of "ME" develops:** 🪞 **Self-recognition** (~18 months): → Rouge test: dot on nose → look in mirror → touch nose → Shows self-awareness has emerged 👶 **Early self-concept** (2-4 years): → Define self by PHYSICAL features and activities → "I'm a girl with brown hair who likes dogs" → All-or-nothing ("I'm good at EVERYTHING") 🏫 **Middle childhood** (6-12): → Social comparison begins → more realistic self-view → Domain-specific self-esteem (academic, social, athletic) → "I'm good at math but bad at sports" 🧑 **Adolescence:** → Abstract self-concepts emerge ("I'm philosophical") → Imaginary audience: "Everyone is watching ME" → Personal fable: "No one understands MY experience" → Looking-glass self (Cooley): we see ourselves as others see us **Harter:** Self-esteem = perceived competence vs. importance of domain → Low self-esteem only when you're bad at things you care about

memoryAids.title: Psychosexual vs Psychosocial Stages
memoryAids.content: **Freud vs. Erikson — Side by side:** | Age | Freud (Psychosexual) | Erikson (Psychosocial) | |---|---|---| | 0-1 | Oral | Trust vs. Mistrust | | 1-3 | Anal | Autonomy vs. Shame | | 3-6 | Phallic | Initiative vs. Guilt | | 6-12 | Latency | Industry vs. Inferiority | | 12-18 | Genital | Identity vs. Role Confusion | | 18-40 | — | Intimacy vs. Isolation | | 40-65 | — | Generativity vs. Stagnation | | 65+ | — | Integrity vs. Despair | **Key differences:** → Freud stopped at adolescence; Erikson = LIFESPAN → Freud = biological drives; Erikson = SOCIAL crises → Freud = psychosexual pleasure zones; Erikson = identity tasks → Both: early experiences shape later personality → Erikson was Freud's student but expanded beyond id/ego/superego

memoryAids.title: Culture-Bound Syndromes
memoryAids.content: **DSM-5 Cultural Concepts of Distress — Key examples:** 🌪️ **Ataque de nervios** (Latin American): → Uncontrollable shouting, crying, trembling, aggression → Triggered by stressful family event → Often resembles panic attack but culturally specific 👻 **Taijin kyofusho** (Japanese): → Fear of offending OTHERS with your appearance/body odor → Opposite of Western social anxiety (fear of being judged) → Reflects collectivist emphasis on not burdening others 🧠 **Dhat syndrome** (South Asian): → Anxiety about loss of semen = loss of vital essence → Fatigue, weakness, guilt 😱 **Khyâl cap** (Cambodian): → "Wind attacks" — panic-like symptoms, fear of wind → Dizziness, palpitations, neck soreness 🌿 **Susto** (Latin American): → "Soul loss" from frightening event → Sadness, sickness, disturbed sleep **DSM-5 approach:** Replaced "culture-bound" with Cultural Formulation Interview (CFI) → Assess cultural CONTEXT, not just symptoms

memoryAids.title: Differential Diagnosis Tips
memoryAids.content: **EPPP Commonly Confused Pairs — How to tell them apart:** 🔄 **MDD vs Bipolar II:** → Ask about HYPOMANIA history — if yes = Bipolar II → Bipolar II patients often present during depression 😰 **GAD vs Normal worry:** → GAD: Excessive, hard to control, 6+ months, 3+ physical symptoms → Normal: Realistic concerns, controllable, time-limited 🔁 **OCD vs OCPD:** → OCD: Ego-DYSTONIC ("I hate these thoughts!") → OCPD: Ego-SYNTONIC ("I just have high standards") 🧠 **Dementia vs Delirium:** → Dementia: GRADUAL onset, clear consciousness, irreversible → Delirium: SUDDEN onset, fluctuating consciousness, often reversible → Delirium = medical emergency! 👤 **Schizoid vs Schizotypal vs Avoidant:** → Schizoid: Doesn't WANT relationships (aloof) → Schizotypal: Odd beliefs + magical thinking → Avoidant: WANTS relationships but fears rejection **Rule of thumb:** When in doubt, look at ONSET, DURATION, and DISTRESS LEVEL

memoryAids.title: Flooding vs Implosion vs Graded Exposure
memoryAids.content: **3 exposure approaches — from most to least intense:** 🌊 **Flooding** = Maximum anxiety, ALL AT ONCE → Real-life (in vivo) exposure to most feared stimulus → Stay until anxiety naturally decreases (habituation) → No relaxation training — just confront the fear! → Fast but can be distressing 💭 **Implosive Therapy** (Stampfl) = Flooding in IMAGINATION → Therapist describes worst-case scenes vividly → May include psychodynamic themes (unconscious conflicts) → Imaginal only — no real exposure → Less commonly used today 📈 **Graded Exposure** = Step by step up a hierarchy → Start with least anxiety-provoking item → Move up only when comfortable at current level → Can be in vivo or imaginal → Most commonly used in practice **Systematic Desensitization** (Wolpe) = Graded exposure + RELAXATION **Key:** All work through extinction/habituation of conditioned fear response

memoryAids.title: Bowen Family Systems Therapy
memoryAids.content: **Murray Bowen — 8 interlocking concepts:** 🔄 **Differentiation of Self** = Core concept! (most important) → Ability to separate THINKING from FEELING → High differentiation = independent, flexible → Low differentiation = fused, reactive, enmeshed → Goal of therapy = increase differentiation 🔺 **Triangles** = 3-person relationship system → When 2 people are stressed → pull in a 3rd → Most stable relationship form (but problematic) → Example: fighting couple involves the child 🧬 **Multigenerational Transmission:** → Patterns repeat across generations → Lower differentiation passed down 📊 **Genogram** = Family diagram showing patterns 🏠 **Nuclear Family Emotional System:** → 4 patterns: conflict, distancing, child focus, dysfunction in a spouse ✂️ **Emotional Cutoff** = Dealing with problems by cutting off contact → Seems like differentiation but it's NOT — it's avoidance **Key:** Bowen is the ONLY family therapist who meets with INDIVIDUALS → Coach one person to change the family system

memoryAids.title: Reality Therapy — Glasser
memoryAids.content: **William Glasser's Choice Theory:** 🧠 **Core idea:** ALL behavior is CHOSEN to meet 5 basic needs → We are NOT victims of our circumstances → We cannot control others — only ourselves 🎯 **5 Basic Needs** — "FLIPS": → **F**reedom (autonomy, independence) → **L**ove & belonging (relationships) → **P**ower (competence, achievement) → **S**urvival (food, shelter, safety) → fun (**F**un & play) 📋 **WDEP System** (intervention): → **W**ants — What do you WANT? → **D**oing — What are you DOING now? → **E**valuation — Is what you're doing WORKING? → **P**lanning — Make a PLAN to do better **Key features:** → NO diagnosis (rejects medical model) → NO discussion of past (focus on present choices) → NO excuses accepted (take responsibility) → Popular in schools, corrections, substance abuse → Therapist is active, directive, encouraging

memoryAids.title: Multivariate Statistics Overview
memoryAids.content: **"When do I use WHAT?" — Quick reference:** 📊 **Multiple Regression** = Predict 1 DV from 2+ IVs → "How well do GRE + GPA predict grad school success?" → β weights show relative contribution of each predictor 📊 **MANOVA** = Compare groups on 2+ DVs simultaneously → "Does therapy type affect BOTH depression AND anxiety?" → Protects against inflated Type I error from multiple ANOVAs 📊 **Discriminant Function Analysis** = Predict GROUP membership → "Which variables best predict diagnostic category?" → Opposite direction of MANOVA 📊 **Canonical Correlation** = Relationship between 2 SETS of variables → Multiple predictors ↔ Multiple outcomes 📊 **Path Analysis / SEM** = Test causal MODELS with multiple variables → Direct and indirect effects → Can test fit of theoretical model to data 📊 **Logistic Regression** = Predict CATEGORICAL outcome → "What predicts pass/fail?" (binary DV) **Rule of thumb:** Count your IVs and DVs to pick the right test!

memoryAids.title: Clinical vs Statistical Prediction
memoryAids.content: **Meehl's (1954) classic finding — still debated!** 🧠 **Clinical prediction** = Expert JUDGMENT → Therapist uses training, intuition, experience → Flexible, considers unique circumstances → But: subject to cognitive biases! 📊 **Statistical (actuarial) prediction** = FORMULA/algorithm → Based on empirical data and base rates → Consistent, objective, reproducible → But: can miss individual nuances 📈 **Meehl's conclusion:** → Statistical prediction is EQUAL OR SUPERIOR in most cases → Meta-analyses confirm: actuarial ≥ clinical for: → Violence prediction, diagnosis, treatment outcome → Clinical prediction wins only when unique data available **Key EPPP points:** → Base rate = prevalence (essential for prediction accuracy) → Clinicians often OVERESTIMATE rare events → Best approach: Use statistical prediction + clinical adjustment → Structured Professional Judgment = combining both

memoryAids.title: Rural & Small Community Ethics
memoryAids.content: **Unique ethical challenges in rural practice:** 🏘️ **Multiple relationships are often UNAVOIDABLE:** → Your client is also your child's teacher → Your client's spouse is your mechanic → You're the ONLY psychologist in 100 miles 📋 **APA guidance:** → Multiple relationships are NOT automatically unethical → Unethical only if they IMPAIR objectivity or risk EXPLOITATION → Document your reasoning for entering the relationship 🛡️ **Best practices:** → Discuss potential complications with the client IN ADVANCE → Consult with colleagues (even by phone/telehealth) → Maintain clear professional boundaries → Consider the client's welfare FIRST → Be mindful of confidentiality in small towns ("I saw you at the therapist's office" = outing) **Key EPPP scenario:** → Rural psychologist asked to see neighbor's child → Best answer: NOT automatic refusal — EVALUATE the situation → Can proceed if no impairment of objectivity → But: NEVER sexual relationships, even in rural areas!

memoryAids.title: Malpractice Elements
memoryAids.content: **"The 4 D's" — ALL 4 required to prove malpractice:** 📋 **D**uty = Professional relationship existed → You were their psychologist (duty of care) → No duty to random strangers (usually) → Exception: Tarasoff duty to identifiable victims ❌ **D**ereliction = You breached the standard of care → Did something a reasonable psychologist would NOT do → OR failed to do something a reasonable psychologist WOULD do → Compared to peers, not perfection 💥 **D**amage = The client was actually HARMED → Emotional, physical, or financial injury → No harm = no malpractice (even if you made a mistake!) 🔗 **D**irect causation = YOUR breach CAUSED the harm → Must prove a direct link between dereliction and damage → Client's pre-existing condition may complicate this **Most common malpractice claims:** → Sexual relationships with clients (#1) → Breach of confidentiality → Failure to warn/protect (Tarasoff) → Improper diagnosis or treatment → Abandonment of client

memoryAids.title: Psychotropic Medication Classes
memoryAids.content: **"AMAS" — 4 major classes of psychotropic meds:** 💊 **A**ntidepressants = Treat depression, anxiety, OCD → SSRIs (fluoxetine/Prozac, sertraline/Zoloft) — 1st line! → SNRIs (venlafaxine/Effexor, duloxetine/Cymbalta) → TCAs (amitriptyline, imipramine) — older, more side effects → MAOIs (phenelzine/Nardil) — tyramine diet restriction! → Onset: 2-6 weeks to take effect 🧠 **M**ood Stabilizers = Bipolar disorder → Lithium = gold standard (needs blood monitoring!) → Valproic acid (Depakote), carbamazepine, lamotrigine → Lithium toxicity: tremor, confusion, seizures 😰 **A**nxiolytics = Anxiety disorders → Benzodiazepines (diazepam/Valium, alprazolam/Xanax) → Fast-acting BUT addictive! (schedule IV) → Buspirone (BuSpar) = non-addictive alternative (slower onset) 🧩 **A**ntipsychotics = Psychotic disorders → Typical/1st gen (haloperidol): block D2 dopamine → EPS risk → Atypical/2nd gen (risperidone, clozapine): fewer EPS → Clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (agranulocytosis risk!)

memoryAids.title: Antidepressant Mechanisms
memoryAids.content: **How each class works — using a bathtub analogy:** 🛁 Think of serotonin/norepinephrine as water in a bathtub (synapse): → Too little water = depression → Goal: keep more water in the tub! 🔌 **SSRIs** = Plug the drain (block reuptake of serotonin) → Selective: ONLY serotonin → Fewest side effects → first-line treatment → Side effects: sexual dysfunction, GI issues, weight gain 🔌🔌 **SNRIs** = Plug TWO drains (serotonin + norepinephrine) → Useful for pain conditions too → "Dual action" antidepressants 🔌🔌🔌 **TCAs** = Plug MULTIPLE drains (less selective) → Block serotonin, NE, AND other receptors → More side effects: cardiac, anticholinergic → Dangerous in overdose (cardiac arrest!) 🚫 **MAOIs** = Turn off the enzyme that BREAKS DOWN water → MAO enzyme normally degrades serotonin, NE, dopamine → MAOIs prevent breakdown → more neurotransmitter available → Tyramine restriction: wine, cheese, aged meats → hypertensive crisis! **Key:** All take 2-6 weeks for full effect (neuroplastic changes)

memoryAids.title: Brain Lobes & Functions
memoryAids.content: **"FTPO" — 4 lobes, front to back:** 🎭 **F**rontal Lobe = The CEO → Executive function, planning, decision-making → Motor cortex (precentral gyrus) = movement → Broca's area (LEFT) = speech PRODUCTION → Personality, impulse control, working memory → Damage: Phineas Gage — personality change, disinhibition 👂 **T**emporal Lobe = The Librarian → Auditory processing → Wernicke's area (LEFT) = language COMPREHENSION → Hippocampus = memory formation → Amygdala = emotional processing (fear!) 🖐️ **P**arietal Lobe = The Navigator → Somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus) = touch, pain, temp → Spatial processing, math → Neglect syndrome: right parietal damage → ignore left side 👁️ **O**ccipital Lobe = The Camera → Visual processing (primary visual cortex) → Damage: cortical blindness, visual agnosia **Lateralization:** → LEFT = language, logic, detail (in most right-handers) → RIGHT = spatial, emotional, holistic, music

memoryAids.title: Aphasia Types
memoryAids.content: **"Broca = Broken speech, Wernicke = Wrong words"** 🗣️ **Broca's Aphasia** (Expressive / Non-fluent): → KNOWS what they want to say but CAN'T produce it → Speech is effortful, halting, telegraphic → "Want... go... store" (grammar stripped out) → Comprehension is INTACT → Location: LEFT frontal lobe (Broca's area) → Often accompanied by right-side paralysis 👂 **Wernicke's Aphasia** (Receptive / Fluent): → Speech FLOWS easily but makes NO sense → "Word salad" — fluent but meaningless → "The shing dog was plocking on the blurner" → Comprehension is IMPAIRED → Location: LEFT temporal lobe (Wernicke's area) → Patient often UNAWARE of their deficit (anosognosia) 🔗 **Conduction Aphasia:** → Can speak AND comprehend but can't REPEAT → Damage to arcuate fasciculus (connects Broca's & Wernicke's) 🌐 **Global Aphasia:** → BOTH production AND comprehension impaired → Most severe form — large left hemisphere damage

memoryAids.title: Motivation Theories — I/O
memoryAids.content: **5 essential workplace motivation theories:** 📊 **Maslow's Hierarchy** = 5 levels (bottom to top): → Physiological → Safety → Belongingness → Esteem → Self-actualization → Must satisfy LOWER needs before higher → Criticism: Too rigid, not well-supported empirically 🔄 **Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory:** → **Hygiene factors** (prevent dissatisfaction): pay, conditions, supervision → **Motivators** (create satisfaction): achievement, recognition, growth → Key: Removing dissatisfaction ≠ creating satisfaction! ⚖️ **Equity Theory** (Adams): → Compare your input/output ratio to others' → Perceived inequity → motivation to restore balance → Underpayment → reduce effort; Overpayment → increase effort 🎯 **Expectancy Theory** (Vroom) — VIE: → **V**alence × **I**nstrumentality × **E**xpectancy = Motivation → Do I value it? Will performance lead to reward? Can I do it? 🏆 **Goal-Setting Theory** (Locke): → SMART goals: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound → Difficult + specific goals → BEST performance

memoryAids.title: Leadership Theories
memoryAids.content: **Evolution of leadership theory — from traits to situations:** 👤 **Trait Theory** = Leaders are BORN (Great Man theory) → Intelligence, dominance, self-confidence → Problem: no universal trait predicts leadership 📋 **Behavioral Theories:** → Ohio State: Initiating Structure vs Consideration → Michigan: Task-oriented vs Employee-oriented → Best leaders score HIGH on both dimensions 🔄 **Contingency/Situational:** → **Fiedler's LPC:** Task-oriented leaders best in HIGH/LOW control → Relationship-oriented best in MODERATE control → Leader style is FIXED — change the situation! → **Hersey-Blanchard:** Match style to follower maturity → Telling → Selling → Participating → Delegating 🌟 **Transformational** (Burns, Bass): → Inspire through vision, charisma, intellectual stimulation → 4 I's: Idealized influence, Inspirational motivation, Intellectual stimulation, Individualized consideration → vs **Transactional** = Reward/punish based on performance 📊 **Leader-Member Exchange (LMX):** → In-group (trusted) vs Out-group (formal only)

memoryAids.title: Job Analysis Methods
memoryAids.content: **"The foundation of all HR functions"** 📋 **What is job analysis?** → Systematic study of tasks, duties, and responsibilities → Foundation for: hiring, training, evaluation, compensation 🔧 **Two orientations:** → **Task-oriented** = WHAT is done (tasks, duties) → **Worker-oriented** = WHO does it (KSAOs) → Knowledge, Skills, Abilities, Other characteristics 📊 **Key methods:** → **PAQ** (Position Analysis Questionnaire) — McCormick → 194 elements, worker-oriented, standardized → Most widely researched instrument → **FJA** (Functional Job Analysis) — Fine → Tasks rated on Data, People, Things → **CIT** (Critical Incident Technique) — Flanagan → Collect examples of effective/ineffective behavior → **O*NET** = Modern replacement for DOT → Online database of occupational info **Job evaluation** = Determine relative WORTH of jobs (for pay) → Different from job analysis (which describes the job)

memoryAids.title: Language Development Milestones
memoryAids.content: **"BCTH-TE" — Language milestones by age:** 👶 **0-2 months: Cooing** → Vowel-like sounds: "oooh" "aaah" 🗣️ **4-6 months: Babbling** → Consonant-vowel combos: "ba-ba" "da-da" → Deaf babies babble too (but it decreases) → Canonical babbling: repeated syllables 👆 **8-12 months: First words** → Holophrases = single word = whole sentence → "Up!" means "Pick me up please!" → Receptive > expressive (understand more than they say) 📝 **18-24 months: Two-word stage** → Telegraphic speech: "Daddy go" "More milk" → Vocabulary explosion (~50 words → 200+ rapidly) → Overextension: all animals = "doggy" → Underextension: only MY dog = "doggy" 🗨️ **3-5 years: Complex sentences** → Overgeneralization: "I goed" "I runned" → This shows RULE learning (not just imitation!) **Theories:** Skinner (learning) vs Chomsky (LAD/nativist) vs Interactionist

memoryAids.title: Vygotsky's Key Concepts
memoryAids.content: **Lev Vygotsky — Social construction of knowledge:** 🎯 **Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD):** → Gap between what child can do ALONE vs with HELP → "Just-right challenge" zone → Learning happens IN the ZPD → Too easy = boredom; Too hard = frustration 🏗️ **Scaffolding** (Wood, Bruner, Ross — not Vygotsky himself!): → Temporary support that is gradually removed → Teacher adjusts help as child gains competence → Like training wheels on a bike 💬 **Private Speech:** → Children talk to themselves to guide behavior → "Now I put the red one here..." → NOT egocentric (Piaget) — it's FUNCTIONAL → Becomes inner speech (internalized) by ~7 🌍 **Social learning precedes individual:** → "Every function appears TWICE: first SOCIAL, then INDIVIDUAL" → Interpsychological → Intrapsychological **Vygotsky vs Piaget:** → V: Social → Individual (culture drives development) → P: Individual → Social (child discovers independently) → V: Language drives thought; P: Thought drives language

memoryAids.title: Personality Assessment Instruments
memoryAids.content: **Big 5 personality assessments for the EPPP:** 📋 **MMPI-3** (Restructured, 2020): → 335 items, True/False → Validity scales: F, L, K (detect faking!) → Clinical scales + RC scales + PSY-5 → Objective, empirically keyed (not theory-based) → Ages 18+ (MMPI-A for adolescents) 🖼️ **Rorschach** (Inkblot): → 10 symmetrical inkblots → Exner's Comprehensive System → now R-PAS → Projective test — measures thought process → Controversial reliability/validity 🖼️ **TAT** (Thematic Apperception Test — Murray): → Ambiguous picture scenes → tell a story → Reveals needs, motives, conflicts (Murray's need theory) → Projective test 📊 **NEO-PI-R** (Costa & McCrae): → Measures Big Five: OCEAN → Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism → 240 items, well-validated **Objective vs Projective:** → Objective: structured, standardized, reliable (MMPI) → Projective: ambiguous, reveals unconscious (Rorschach, TAT)

memoryAids.title: Neuropsychological Assessment
memoryAids.content: **Key neuropsych batteries & tests for EPPP:** 🧠 **Halstead-Reitan Battery:** → Comprehensive, fixed battery (same tests for everyone) → Impairment Index: 0-1 (higher = more impairment) → Includes Category Test, TPT, Finger Tapping → Good for lateralization (left vs right damage) 🧠 **Luria-Nebraska Battery:** → Briefer than Halstead-Reitan → Based on Luria's theory of brain function → 11 scales including motor, rhythm, visual → Better for qualitative analysis 📊 **Individual tests:** → **Wisconsin Card Sorting Test (WCST):** → Measures executive function / cognitive flexibility → Sensitive to FRONTAL LOBE damage → Perseveration = key error pattern → **Trail Making Test (A & B):** → Part A: connect numbers (processing speed) → Part B: alternate numbers & letters (set shifting) → Part B sensitive to frontal lobe function → **Bender-Gestalt** (Bender Visual Motor Gestalt Test): → Copy geometric designs → Screens for brain damage + development → Also used with children (developmental level)

memoryAids.title: Group Therapy — Yalom's Therapeutic Factors
memoryAids.content: **Irvin Yalom's 11 Therapeutic Factors in group therapy:** 🌟 **"HUGE CAIRS" + 3 more:** → **H**ope installation = Seeing others improve → **U**niversality = "I'm not the only one!" → **G**roup cohesiveness = Bonding, belonging → **E**xistential factors = Accepting life's limitations → **C**atharsis = Emotional release/expression → **A**ltruism = Helping others helps YOU → **I**nterpersonal learning = Feedback from group members → **R**ecapitulation of family = Group recreates family dynamics → **S**ocializing techniques = Learn social skills → **I**mitative behavior = Model after others → **I**mparting information = Psychoeducation **Stages of group (Tuckman):** → Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning **Key:** Universality and hope are especially important EARLY Cohesion is the group equivalent of therapeutic alliance

memoryAids.title: Solution-Focused Brief Therapy
memoryAids.content: **Steve de Shazer & Insoo Kim Berg — Key techniques:** ✨ **Miracle Question:** → "If a miracle happened tonight and your problem was solved..." → "...what would be DIFFERENT tomorrow?" → Shifts focus from problem to SOLUTION → Helps client envision desired future 📊 **Scaling Questions:** → "On a scale of 1-10, where are you now?" → "What would it take to move from a 5 to a 6?" → Makes progress concrete and measurable 🔍 **Exception Finding:** → "When is the problem NOT happening?" → Find times when it's already better → do MORE of that → Exceptions = existing strengths/solutions 👏 **Compliments & Coping Questions:** → "How have you managed to cope so well?" → Highlight existing strengths and resilience **Key principles:** → Clients are the EXPERTS on their own lives → Focus on what's WORKING, not what's broken → Small changes lead to big changes → Brief: typically 3-5 sessions → No formal diagnosis needed; atheoretical about cause

memoryAids.title: DBT — Dialectical Behavior Therapy
memoryAids.content: **Marsha Linehan — Originally for Borderline Personality Disorder:** 💡 **Core dialectic:** Acceptance AND Change (both/and, not either/or) 🎯 **4 Skills modules — "DIME":** 🧘 **D**istress Tolerance = Survive crisis without making it worse → TIPP: Temperature, Intense exercise, Paced breathing, Paired muscle relaxation → Radical acceptance: "It is what it is" 🤝 **I**nterpersonal Effectiveness = Get what you need in relationships → DEAR MAN: Describe, Express, Assert, Reinforce, Mindful, Appear confident, Negotiate → Balance priorities, self-respect, relationships 🧠 **M**indfulness = Core skill (foundation for all others) → Observe, Describe, Participate → Non-judgmentally, One-mindfully, Effectively → "Wise mind" = intersection of emotion mind + reasonable mind 😊 **E**motion Regulation = Reduce vulnerability to negative emotions → ABC PLEASE: Accumulate positives, Build mastery, Cope ahead → PLEASE: Physical health (treat PhysicaL illness, balanced Eating, avoid mood-Altering substances, balanced Sleep, Exercise) **Treatment structure:** Individual therapy + Skills group + Phone coaching + Consultation team

memoryAids.title: Type I vs Type II Errors
memoryAids.content: **"The Boy Who Cried Wolf" analogy:** 🐺 **Type I Error (α / Alpha) = FALSE ALARM** → Reject H₀ when it's actually TRUE → "Crying wolf" — saying there's an effect when there ISN'T → Conclusion: "It works!" Reality: "No, it doesn't." → Controlled by setting alpha level (usually .05) → p < .05 means: "Less than 5% chance this is a Type I error" 😴 **Type II Error (β / Beta) = MISS** → Fail to reject H₀ when it's actually FALSE → "Missing the wolf" — saying there's NO effect when there IS → Conclusion: "It doesn't work." Reality: "Yes, it does." → Related to statistical POWER (1 − β) ⚡ **Power** = Probability of correctly detecting a real effect → Increase power by: larger N, larger effect, higher α, less variability → Typical goal: power ≥ .80 (80%) **Tradeoff:** Reducing Type I risk → increases Type II risk (and vice versa) **Mnemonic:** Type I = False Positive (I = "I see something that's not there") Type II = False Negative (II = "It's there but I missed it")

memoryAids.title: Effect Size Interpretation
memoryAids.content: **"Small, Medium, Large" — Cohen's benchmarks:** 📊 **Cohen's d** (mean difference in SD units): → Small = 0.2 (barely noticeable) → Medium = 0.5 (noticeable in everyday life) → Large = 0.8 (very obvious difference) → d = (M₁ - M₂) / SD_pooled 📊 **Pearson's r** (correlation / effect size): → Small = .10 (r² = 1% variance explained) → Medium = .30 (r² = 9% variance explained) → Large = .50 (r² = 25% variance explained) 📊 **Eta-squared (η²)** (for ANOVA): → Small = .01 → Medium = .06 → Large = .14 → Proportion of DV variance explained by IV **Why effect size matters:** → Statistical significance ≠ practical significance! → Large N can make TINY effects "significant" → APA requires reporting effect size (since 2001) → Meta-analyses use effect sizes to combine studies **r² = coefficient of determination** = % of variance explained → r = .50 → r² = .25 → accounts for 25% of variance

memoryAids.title: Informed Consent Elements
memoryAids.content: **"PRINCDT" — 7 essential elements of informed consent:** 📋 **P**urpose of treatment/evaluation → What will we be doing and why? ⏰ **R**isks and benefits → Potential harms AND potential gains → Must disclose REASONABLY FORESEEABLE risks 🔒 **I**nformation about confidentiality → What's private and what's NOT (limits!) → Mandated reporting, Tarasoff, subpoenas 💰 **N**ature of fees and billing → Session cost, insurance, cancellation policy → Collection procedures for unpaid balances 🚪 **C**hoice — voluntary participation → Right to refuse or withdraw at ANY time → Without penalty or negative consequences 📞 **D**uration and alternatives → Expected length of treatment → Alternative treatment options available ❓ **T**herapist qualifications → Training, licensure, areas of competence → Supervision status if trainee **Special populations:** → Minors: parent/guardian consents; child gives ASSENT → Court-ordered: still need IC but limits on confidentiality differ → Cognitive impairment: consent from legal guardian

memoryAids.title: Supervision Models
memoryAids.content: **3 major supervision models for EPPP:** 📋 **Developmental Models:** → **IDM** (Integrated Developmental Model — Stoltenberg): → Level 1: Anxious, rigid, imitative (need structure) → Level 2: Fluctuating confidence, autonomous struggles → Level 3: Stable identity, flexible, "master clinician" → Level 3i: Integrated across domains → Supervisor adjusts style to match supervisee's level 🔄 **Discrimination Model** (Bernard): → 3 Roles: Teacher, Counselor, Consultant → 3 Focus areas: Process, Conceptualization, Personalization → Supervisor shifts roles based on what's needed → Most flexible/atheoretical model 🧠 **Psychotherapy-Based Models:** → Match supervision to therapy orientation → Psychodynamic: parallel process, transference in supervision → CBT: structured, skill-focused, homework → Person-centered: empathy, genuineness in supervision **Key ethics of supervision:** → Supervisor has VICARIOUS LIABILITY for supervisee's clients → Supervisor must be competent in supervisee's treatment methods → Dual relationships with supervisees: power differential!

memoryAids.title: Personality Disorder Clusters
memoryAids.content: **"Mad, Bad, Sad" — the 3 clusters:** 🌀 **Cluster A — ODD / Eccentric ("Mad"):** → **Paranoid** = Suspicious, distrustful, reads malice everywhere → **Schizoid** = Loner, flat affect, doesn't WANT relationships → **Schizotypal** = Odd beliefs, magical thinking, eccentric → Think: related to schizophrenia spectrum 🔥 **Cluster B — DRAMATIC / Erratic ("Bad"):** → **Antisocial** = Disregard for rights of others, deceit, no remorse → Must be 18+; Conduct Disorder before age 15 → **Borderline** = Unstable relationships, identity, emotions; fear of abandonment → Splitting, self-harm, chronic emptiness → **Histrionic** = Attention-seeking, excessive emotion, theatrical → **Narcissistic** = Grandiosity, need for admiration, lack of empathy 😰 **Cluster C — ANXIOUS / Fearful ("Sad"):** → **Avoidant** = WANTS relationships but fears rejection (shy) → **Dependent** = Clingy, submissive, can't make decisions alone → **Obsessive-Compulsive PD** = Rigid, perfectionist, controlling → NOT same as OCD! (OCPD is ego-syntonic) **Most commonly tested:** Borderline, Antisocial, Narcissistic

memoryAids.title: Schizophrenia Spectrum
memoryAids.content: **Positive vs Negative symptoms + related disorders:** ➕ **Positive symptoms** (added to normal experience): → Hallucinations (auditory most common — hearing voices) → Delusions (persecutory most common) → Disorganized speech (loose associations, tangentiality) → Disorganized/catatonic behavior → Respond BETTER to medication (antipsychotics) ➖ **Negative symptoms** (subtracted from normal): → Flat affect (reduced emotional expression) → Alogia (poverty of speech) → Avolition (lack of motivation/initiative) → Anhedonia (can't feel pleasure) → Asociality (social withdrawal) → HARDER to treat, predict WORSE prognosis 📊 **DSM-5 Spectrum:** → Schizophrenia: 6+ months, 1+ month active symptoms → Schizophreniform: 1-6 months (same symptoms) → Brief Psychotic Disorder: < 1 month → Schizoaffective: schizophrenia + mood episode → Delusional Disorder: delusions ONLY (no other symptoms) **Dopamine hypothesis:** Excess dopamine in mesolimbic pathway → positive symptoms Too LITTLE dopamine in mesocortical pathway → negative symptoms

memoryAids.title: Sleep Stages
memoryAids.content: **NREM → REM cycle (~90 minutes):** 😴 **Stage 1 (N1):** Light sleep (5%) → Theta waves → Hypnagogic hallucinations (vivid images) → Myoclonic jerks ("falling" sensation) → Easy to wake up 💤 **Stage 2 (N2):** True sleep onset (45-50%) → Sleep spindles + K-complexes → Spindles = memory consolidation! → Most of the night spent here 🌊 **Stage 3 (N3):** Deep/Slow-Wave Sleep (15-20%) → Delta waves (slow, high amplitude) → MOST restorative physically → Growth hormone released → Hardest to wake up → Parasomnias occur here: sleepwalking, night terrors, bedwetting 🎬 **REM Sleep** (20-25%): → Rapid eye movements, vivid DREAMS → Muscle atonia (paralyzed — prevents acting out dreams) → EEG looks like AWAKE brain (paradoxical sleep) → Memory consolidation (procedural + emotional) → REM increases across the night → REM rebound: REM deprivation → MORE REM next night **Key:** Infants ~50% REM; elderly less deep sleep (N3)

memoryAids.title: Split-Brain Research
memoryAids.content: **Sperry & Gazzaniga — What happens when you cut the corpus callosum?** ✂️ **Corpus callosum** = Bridge connecting left & right hemispheres → Cut to treat severe epilepsy (split-brain surgery) → Each hemisphere works INDEPENDENTLY after surgery 👁️ **Visual field trick:** → LEFT visual field → RIGHT hemisphere → RIGHT visual field → LEFT hemisphere → (Optic chiasm crosses the info) 🧪 **Classic experiment:** → Flash "KEY" to LEFT visual field (goes to RIGHT hemisphere) → Patient says: "I didn't see anything" (LEFT hemisphere speaks) → BUT: Left hand picks up a key! (RIGHT hemisphere controls left hand) 🧠 **Lateralization summary:** → **LEFT hemisphere:** Language, logic, math, analysis → Broca's & Wernicke's areas → "The interpreter" (makes up explanations) → **RIGHT hemisphere:** Spatial, emotion, music, face recognition → Holistic processing, creativity → Better at detecting deception **Key:** Split-brain patients can have conflicts between hemispheres → "Alien hand syndrome" — one hand acts against the other!

memoryAids.title: Signal Detection Theory
memoryAids.content: **SDT — Beyond simple thresholds:** 📊 **4 possible outcomes when detecting a signal:** | | Signal Present | Signal Absent | |---|---|---| | Say "Yes" | ✅ HIT | ❌ FALSE ALARM | | Say "No" | ❌ MISS | ✅ CORRECT REJECTION | 🎯 **Two key factors:** → **Sensitivity (d')** = Ability to distinguish signal from noise → Higher d' = better at detecting signals → Determined by signal strength and observer ability → **Response bias (β)** = Tendency to say "yes" or "no" → Liberal bias: say "yes" often (more hits BUT more false alarms) → Conservative bias: say "no" often (fewer false alarms BUT more misses) 📋 **Real-world examples:** → Radiologist reading X-rays (tumor or not?) → Eyewitness lineup (is the perpetrator there?) → Airport security (threat or harmless?) **Key:** Performance depends on BOTH sensitivity AND decision criteria → Not just about ability — motivation and consequences matter!

memoryAids.title: Schedules of Reinforcement
memoryAids.content: **"FVFV" — 4 partial reinforcement schedules:** ⏰ **Fixed Ratio (FR)** = Reinforce after set NUMBER of responses → Example: Pay per 10 widgets assembled → Pattern: Pause after reinforcement, then rapid responding → "Post-reinforcement pause" 🎰 **Variable Ratio (VR)** = Reinforce after AVERAGE number of responses → Example: Slot machines, fishing, sales calls → Pattern: HIGHEST + STEADIEST response rate! → MOST resistant to extinction → "You never know when the next one will pay off" ⏱️ **Fixed Interval (FI)** = Reinforce first response after set TIME → Example: Checking mail, weekly paycheck → Pattern: "Scallop" — slow start, speeds up near interval end → Studying intensifies before exam 🎲 **Variable Interval (VI)** = Reinforce first response after AVERAGE time → Example: Checking email, pop quizzes → Pattern: Slow, STEADY responding → Very resistant to extinction **Key:** Variable schedules = MORE resistant to extinction Ratio schedules = HIGHER response rates **Partial Reinforcement Extinction Effect (PREE):** Partial > Continuous for resistance

memoryAids.title: Bandura's Observational Learning
